7.13: hazeh haveit bechezwe leylaya vaara im-anane shemayya kebar enash ateh vead attiq yomaya matah uqdamoy haytivuhi (Aramaic): 'I saw in the night visions, and behold, with the clouds of heaven there came one like a son of man, and he came to the Ancient of Days and was presented before him.' The 'one like a son of man' (kebar enash, Aramaic for 'like a human being') in Daniel 7 contrasts with the four beasts (lions, bears, leopards, a terrible beast) that rise from the sea — representing successive human empires. The human figure comes from heaven, not the sea, and receives the dominion the beasts claimed. The NT application (Jesus's self-designation as 'Son of Man' in all four Gospels) is the consistent claim that Jesus is this figure who receives eternal dominion from the Ancient of Days — a claim recognized as divine by the Sanhedrin (Mark 14:62-64).
7.1: ḥĕlem ḥĕzî rēʾšēh (חֵלֶם חֶזְוֵי רֵאשֵׁהּ): 'a dream and visions of his head.' Ḥĕlem (dream, Aramaic) parallels Hebrew חֲלוֹם. Ḥĕzwāʾ (Aramaic, plural: visions) is from the root ḥzh = to see, the standard Aramaic visionary vocabulary. Daniel writes the dream (kĕtab ḥĕlmāʾ) — the narrative self-archiving of the vision, an act of prophetic accountability. The mention of 'chief of the matters' (rêš millîn) signals that Daniel summarizes the dream's essence.
7.2: ʾarbaʿ rûḥîn dî-šĕmayyāʾ mĕgîḥān (אַרְבַּע רוּחִין דִּי-שְׁמַיָּא מְגִיחָן): 'the four winds of heaven stirring.' Rûḥîn = winds/spirits (Aramaic plural of rûaḥ). Mĕgîḥān from gîḥ = to rush out, burst forth. The 'great sea' (yammāʾ rabbāʾ) is the Mediterranean in the literal sense, but functions symbolically as chaotic primordial waters (Gen 1:2; Isa 27:1). Revelation 7:1 picks up the four winds held back by angels as the same cosmic force that precedes the sealing of God's servants.
7.3: ʾarbaʿ ḥêwān rabbĕwān sāleqan min-yammāʾ: 'four great beasts came up from the sea.' Ḥêwāh (Aramaic) = beast, living creature — cognate with Hebrew חַיָּה. Beasts rising from the sea echoes the Canaanite chaos-myth (Yam, the sea-dragon) and Isa 27:1 (Leviathan). The four beasts represent four world-empires, confirmed in 7:17: 'These four great beasts are four kings who shall arise out of the earth.'
7.4: ʾaryēh wĕgappîn dî-nĕšar lēh (אַרְיֵה וְגַפִּין דִּי-נְשַׁר לֵהּ): 'a lion with eagle's wings.' ʾAryēh (lion) + nĕšar (eagle) — Babylon's two royal symbols in both Neo-Babylonian iconography and the OT (Jer 4:13; Hab 1:8). The plucking of wings (mĕrîṭ) and being made to stand like a man (kĕʾĕnāš qāmat) and receiving a human heart (lĕbab ʾĕnāš yĕhîb lēh) tracks with Nebuchadnezzar's humiliation and restoration in chs. 3-4.
7.5: dāmyāh lĕdubb (דָּמְיָה לְדֹב): 'resembling a bear.' The comparative particle + animal comparison is the standard beast-vision idiom. The bear raised on one side (lĕsitar-ḥad hûqĕmat) is commonly interpreted as Medo-Persia, with one side elevated because Persia dominated Media. The three ribs (tĕlātāh ʿillʿîn) in its mouth are typically identified with three conquered kingdoms; the command 'arise, devour much flesh' captures Persia's vast territorial expansion.
7.6: gappîn dî-ʿôp ʿal gabbēh (גַּפִּין דִּי-עוֹף עַל-גַּבֵּהּ): 'wings of a bird on its back.' The leopard (nĕmar) with four wings and four heads represents Greece under Alexander and his four successor-generals (the Diadochi) — confirmed by 8:8, 22. The swiftness of Alexander's conquest is captured in the image of a leopard already fast, with four wings added. Šoltān (dominion) was given to it — the same word used for the Son of Man's dominion in 7:14, but here provisional.
7.7: dĕḥîlāh wĕʾêmtānāh wĕtaqqîpāh yattîrāʾ: 'dreadful, terrible, and exceedingly strong.' Three Aramaic adjectives pile up: dĕḥîl (feared), ʾêmtan (terrifying), taqqîp yattîr (exceedingly mighty). The fourth beast is unnamed — unlike the lion, bear, and leopard, no animal analogy suffices for Rome's universal and unprecedented dominion. It has iron teeth (šinnayyin dî-parzel) and ten horns, and tramples everything.
7.8: qeren ʾoḥŏrî zĕʿêrāh (קֶרֶן אָחֳרִי זְעֵירָה): 'another horn, a small one.' Qeren (horn, Aramaic and Hebrew) is the standard symbol of power/ruler throughout the OT. The 'little horn' starts small (zĕʿêr) but speaks grandiose things. Eyes like human eyes (ʿênîn kĕʿênê ʾĕnāšāʾ) suggest intelligence; a mouth speaking great things (pum mĕmalil rabrĕbān) is its primary characteristic. This figure forms the background for the NT's 'man of lawlessness' and 'antichrist' traditions.
7.9: ʿattîq yômayāʾ yĕtib (עַתִּיק יוֹמַיָּא יְתִב): 'the Ancient of Days took his seat.' ʿAttîq (Aramaic) = ancient, advanced in days — a title used only three times in Daniel (7:9, 13, 22) and nowhere else in Scripture. The description that follows — garment white as snow (lĕbûšēh kĕtlag ḥiwr), hair like pure wool (śar rēʾšēh kĕʿamar nĕqēʾ), throne of fiery flames (kursyēh šĕbîbîn dî-nûr), wheels of burning fire — is the direct source for Revelation 1:13-15's description of Christ.
7.10: nĕhar dî-nûr nāgēd wĕnāpēq min-qŏdāmôhî: 'a stream of fire issued and came out from before him.' The fiery stream (nĕhar dî-nûr) is the river of divine judgment from the throne. Revelation 22:1 transforms this into the river of life flowing from the throne — the same stream carries either judgment or life depending on one's relationship to the enthroned one. The 'ten thousand times ten thousand' standing before him (ribbô ribbĕwān) becomes the number of the heavenly host in Rev 5:11.
7.11: bĕqōl millayāʾ rabbĕrĕbātāʾ: 'because of the sound of the great words.' The fourth beast's destruction is specifically caused by its speech: the great-sounding mouth is the proximate cause of its slaying. Qĕtal (slain) + yĕqad bĕnûrāʾ (burnt in fire) matches 2 Thess 2:8: the lawless one 'the Lord will consume with the breath of his mouth and bring to nothing by the appearance of his coming.'
7.12: ʿiddānĕhôn ûzimānĕhôn yĕhalûn: 'their seasons and times were prolonged.' The three defeated beasts have their dominion taken away but their lives extended for 'a season and a time.' ʿIddan (Aramaic) = time, season — the same word used in 7:25 for 'time, times, and half a time.' The contrast between the fourth beast's total destruction and the others' sustained existence (minus dominion) suggests a graduated pattern of divine judgment.
7.14: šāltān wîqār ûmalkû (שָׁלְטָן וִיקָר וּמַלְכוּ): 'dominion and glory and a kingdom.' Three Aramaic throne-terms given to the Son of Man: šāltān (sovereignty/dominion), yĕqār (honor/glory), malkû (kingdom). The triad reappears in Revelation 5:12 as the acclamation of the Lamb. The universality clause — kol-ʿammayyāʾ ʾummayāʾ wĕlišānayāʾ — is the same tripartite peoples-nations-languages formula from throughout Daniel, now applied to the Son of Man's eternal dominion.
7.15: ʾitkaryat rûḥî ʾĕnāh dānîyēʾl bĕgaw nidnāh: 'my spirit was anxious within me in its sheath.' Kĕrāh (to be anxious, contracted) describes Daniel's interior state. The word nidnāh (sheath/covering) is debated: most translate 'in its sheath' — the spirit within its bodily covering. The image of the spirit as something held within the body-sheath reflects Aramaic anthropology where rûaḥ is the animating interior person.
7.16: yaddaʿ ʾôdaʿinnî: 'he made me know definitively.' The doubled causative form of ydʿ (know, Aramaic Aphel) means the angel causes Daniel to know certainly. The pattern — visionary overwhelmed, angelic interpretation follows — is the standard apocalyptic schema developed from Ezekiel 1-2 and formalized here in Daniel 7, influencing Revelation's entire interpretive structure.
7.17: ʾinnûn ʾarbaʿāh malkîn dî-min-ʾarʿāʾ yĕqûmûn: 'these four great beasts are four kings who shall arise from the earth.' The interpretive key: beasts = kings/kingdoms arising from ʾarʿāʾ (earth). The source is earth (ʾarʿāʾ), in contrast to the Son of Man who comes from heaven's clouds. Earth-origin vs. heaven-descent is the structural contrast of the whole vision.
7.18: qaddîšê ʿelyônîn (קַדִּישֵׁי עֶלְיוֹנִין): 'the saints of the Most High.' Qaddîš (Aramaic) = holy one, saint — the same word used for angelic 'watchers' (qaddîšîn, 4:13, 17). ʿElyônîn = the Most High (plural of majesty for God in Aramaic). The promise — 'shall receive the kingdom and possess the kingdom forever' — uses the same malkû (kingdom) given to the Son of Man (v. 14). The saints share in what the Son of Man receives; their possession flows from his.
7.19: dî hăwāt šĕniyyāh min-kol-lĕhon: 'which was different from all the others.' Šĕnāh (Aramaic, passive: to be different/changed) — the fourth beast is qualitatively different, not just another variant. Iron teeth and bronze claws are added to the earlier description; the trampling is emphasized twice. This beast's un-nameable character signals that it exceeds the existing taxonomy of power.
7.20: qarnāʾ dî-ʿênayyin hăwāt bāh wĕpum mĕmalil rabrĕbān: 'the horn that had eyes and a mouth speaking great things.' The little horn's distinguishing features — eyes (ʿênayyin = intelligence) and mouth (pum mĕmalil) — are cognitive and rhetorical. It is a horn that thinks and speaks rather than merely exercises power. Revelation 13:5-6 describes the beast from the sea with 'a mouth uttering haughty and blasphemous words' — drawing directly from this passage.
7.21: qarnāʾ dikkēn ʿābĕdāh qĕrāb ʿim-qaddîšîn wĕyāklāh lĕhôn: 'the same horn made war with the saints and prevailed over them.' ʿAbĕdāh qĕrāb (made war) — the warfare vocabulary of Daniel echoes Revelation 13:7: 'It was allowed to make war on the saints and to conquer them.' The prevailing of the little horn over the saints is temporary — the Ancient of Days' arrival (v. 22) ends it — but the interim domination is real and its duration is measured.
7.22: ʿad dî-ʾătāh ʿattîq yômayāʾ wĕdînāʾ yĕhib lĕqaddîšê ʿelyônîn: 'until the Ancient of Days came and judgment was given in favor of the saints of the Most High.' The Aramaic dînāʾ yĕhib lĕ- means 'judgment was rendered in favor of' — the same phrase in legal contexts means 'a verdict for' the plaintiff. The arrival of the Ancient of Days turns the court verdict: from prevailing horn to holy-ones-vindicated.
7.23: malkûtāʾ rĕbîʿāytāʾ (מַלְכוּתָא רְבִיעָיְתָא): 'a fourth kingdom on earth.' The angel confirms beast = kingdom. The fourth kingdom will tēʾkul kol-ʾarʿāʾ (devour the whole earth) and tĕdûšinnāh wĕtaddiqinnāh (trample and crush it) — three verbs of total dominance. The scale of the fourth kingdom's destructive power accounts for the unprecedented terror of its symbolic form.
7.24: wĕʾoḥŏran lēh yĕqûm: 'and another shall arise after them.' The little horn arises ʾoḥĕran (after, different) from the ten, humbles three kings, and is therefore both sequential and qualitatively other. The 'humbling of three kings' (wĕtĕlātāh malkîn yĕšappil) has generated various historical identifications but functions literarily as a marker of unprecedented political disruption within the final empire.
7.25: ʿiddān wĕʿiddānîn ûpĕlag ʿiddān (עִדָּן וְעִדָּנִין וּפְלַג עִדָּן): 'a time, and times, and half a time.' The most contested chronological formula in Daniel. ʿIddān = time/year (Aramaic); ʿiddānîn = times; pĕlag ʿiddān = half a time. The formula = 1 + 2 + ½ = 3½ units. This corresponds to the 42 months / 1,260 days of Revelation 12:14; 11:2-3; 13:5 — the period of the great tribulation in apocalyptic reckoning. The little horn speaks great words against the Most High and 'wears out the saints' (yĕballē qaddîšîn) during this period.
7.26: wĕdînāʾ yitib wĕšāltānēh yĕhaddĕmûn: 'but the court shall sit in judgment and his dominion shall be taken away.' The heavenly court that earlier gave judgment in favor of the saints (v. 22) now acts to annihilate the little horn's dominion. Šāltān taken away, consumed, and finally destroyed — three stages: removal of authority, ongoing destruction (yahăbĕdûn), and terminal annihilation (ʿad-sôpāʾ).
7.27: malkûtāʾ wĕšāltānāʾ wĕrabbûtāʾ: 'the kingdom and the dominion and the greatness of the kingdoms under the whole heaven.' The third and final occurrence of the throne-vocabulary triad (malkût + šāltān + now rabbût, greatness). 'Under the whole heaven' — the domain previously claimed by the four beasts is now given to the saints. The eternal kingdom (malkûtēh malkût ʿālam) serves the Son of Man; the saints' kingdom is the visible administration of what the Son of Man receives in vv. 13-14.
7.28: sôpāʾ pĕtgāmāʾ (סוֹפָא פִּתְגָמָא): 'here is the end of the matter.' Sôpāʾ (Aramaic) = end, conclusion. Daniel 7:28 ends the Aramaic block that began at 2:4b — the final verse of the Aramaic section is marked by personal distress (rĕʿyônay yĕballĕhunnannî, 'my thoughts alarmed me') and preserved silence (millĕtāʾ bĕlibbî nĕṭarēt, 'I kept the matter in my heart'). The changed countenance (zîwî šĕnaw ʿălî) is a standard theophanic response.

9.24: shivim shavuim nechetach al-amecha vehal ir qadshecha lekale happesha ulehatem chataut velchapper avon ulehavi tsdeq olamim velachtom chazot venavia velimshoach qodesh qodashim: 'Seventy weeks are decreed about your people and your holy city, to finish the transgression, to put an end to sin, to atone for iniquity, to bring in everlasting righteousness, to seal both vision and prophet, and to anoint a most holy place.' The six infinitives of Dan 9:24 have generated centuries of calculation and debate. The shavuim (weeks/sevens) are most naturally weeks of years (seven-year units), giving 490 years from the decree to rebuild Jerusalem. The six goals — which are systematically soteriological and eschatological — align most naturally with Christ's work: atonement (to finish transgression, atone for iniquity), righteousness (bring in everlasting righteousness), and the end of the prophetic age (seal vision and prophet).
9.1: bishnat achat leDaryavesh ben Achashverosh mizzera Madai (bišnat ʾaḥat lĕdāryāwēš ben ʾăḥašwērôš mizzeraʿ māday): 'In the first year of Darius son of Ahasuerus, of Median descent.' The dating formula is precise: Darius (identity debated — either a secondary title for Cyrus or an otherwise unrecorded official) is identified by parentage and ethnicity. Mizzera Madai (of Median seed) situates Daniel's prayer in the Medo-Persian transition, the moment when the seventy-year prophecy of Jeremiah (9:2) is approaching fulfillment. The historical precision grounds the prayer in real time — Daniel calculates the prophetic clock.
9.2: binah b'sefarim (bînāh bĕsĕpārîm): 'understood from the scrolls/books.' Binah (H998) is not merely reading but interpretive understanding — the same root as the heavenly figure's instruction to Daniel to understand (hitbonen, v.23). Sefarim (scrolls) refers to the written prophetic corpus already circulating as authoritative texts; Daniel treats Jeremiah's prophecy as canonical Scripture. Shivim shanah (seventy years): Jer 25:11-12 and 29:10 — Daniel is calculating the prophetic timeline. This is one of the earliest explicit examples of prophetic interpretation of prior prophecy, establishing the tradition of inner-biblical exegesis.
9.3: etten et-panai el-Adonai ha'Elohim (ʾettēn ʾet-pānay ʾel-ʾădōnāy hāʾĕlōhîm): 'I set my face toward the Lord God.' Natan panim (set the face) is a Hebrew idiom for deliberate, focused orientation — the whole self directed toward God in covenantal address. The prayer disciplines listed — tsom (fasting), saq (sackcloth), efer (ashes) — are the classic triad of covenant mourning, mirroring Jonah 3:6 and Esther 4:1-3. Daniel mourns over the covenant's broken condition before he prays for its restoration, establishing penitence as the necessary posture for effective intercessory prayer.
9.4: el gadol venora shomer haberit vehahesed (ʾēl gādôl wĕnôrāʾ šômēr habbĕrît wĕhahesed): 'great and awesome God who keeps the covenant and steadfast love.' Daniel's prayer opens with YHWH's covenant-character as the basis for appeal — not human merit but divine attribute. Nora (awesome/fearsome): the same term used in Exod 34:10; Deut 10:21. Shomer haberit vehahesed: the covenant-keeping formula from the Deuteronomic blessing tradition (Deut 7:9, 12), invoked here as the ground of Daniel's hope. Prayer appeals to who God is, not what Israel deserves.
9.5: chatanu va'avinu hirshanu umradnu vesor mimitzvotecha (ḥāṭāʾnû wĕʿāwînû hirŝaʿnû ûmāradnû wĕsôr mimmitswōtêkā): 'We have sinned and done wrong and acted wickedly and rebelled and turned aside from your commandments.' Daniel deploys five Hebrew sin-verbs in rapid sequence — one of the most comprehensive sin-catalogues in Scripture. Chata (H2398, miss the mark), avah (H5753, twist/pervert), hirshia (H7561, act wickedly/be in the wrong), marad (H4775, rebel openly), sur (H5493, turn aside/deviate). This is not rhetorical accumulation but theological precision: sin is simultaneously moral failure, relational distortion, covenantal rebellion, and directional deviation.
9.6: velo shamanu el-avadecha haneviim (wĕlōʾ šāmaʿnû ʾel-ʿăbādêkā hannĕbîʾîm): 'We did not listen to your servants the prophets.' Shama (H8085) is simultaneously to hear and to obey — non-listening is non-obedience. The phrase avadecha haneviim (your servants the prophets) is the characteristic Deuteronomistic designation for the prophetic office (2 Kgs 9:7; 17:13, 23; Amos 3:7; Jer 7:25). The chain — YHWH sent, prophets spoke, Israel refused to hear — identifies the theological mechanism of the exile: covenantal deafness to the divine word.
9.7: lecha Adonai hatsedaqah velanu bosheth hapanim (lĕkā ʾădōnāy haṣṣĕdāqāh wĕlānû bôšet happānîm): 'To you, Lord, belongs righteousness; to us shame of face.' The antithesis is theologically precise: tsedaqah (H6666) here is YHWH's covenant-faithfulness and rectifying justice — the quality by which he acts consistently with his own covenant character. Bosheth hapanim (shame of face) is a Semitic idiom for public disgrace and dishonor. The exile is the visible manifestation of this shame — Israel's covenantal failure exposed before the nations. Daniel prays not by hiding this but by owning it as the starting point for covenantal appeal.
9.8: lanu bosheth hapanim lamelachim leshareinu uleavoteinu asher chatanu lach: 'To us belongs shame of face — to our kings, our officials, and our fathers who sinned against you.' The scope of the confession is comprehensive: shame belongs to every stratum of Israelite society — monarchy, administration, and the ancestral heritage. Daniel does not exempt the leadership from the communal confession; the exile is a systemic failure, not merely individual lapses. The solidarity of confession — chatanu lach (we sinned against you) — is the model of corporate representative prayer that shapes later Jewish liturgy.
9.9: laAdonai Eloheinu harachamim vehaselichot ki maradnu bo (laʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhênû hāraḥămîm wĕhasslîḥôt kî māradnû bô): 'To the Lord our God belong mercy and forgiveness, for we have rebelled against him.' Rachamim (H7356, mercies/compassions): plural intensive of rechem (womb) — YHWH's compassion is as intimate and unconditional as a mother's for her child (Isa 49:15). Selichot (H5547, forgivenesses): plural, occurring only three times in the OT (Neh 9:17; Ps 130:4; here) — the plural emphasizes the inexhaustible character of divine forgiveness. The logic is daring: because we have rebelled, YHWH's forgiveness-quality is now the only hope.
9.10: velo shamanu beqol YHWH Eloheinu lalechet betorotav: 'We did not listen to the voice of YHWH our God by walking in his laws.' The phrase lalechet (to walk) marks Torah-observance as a mode of life — halakha (from halakh, walk) later becomes the rabbinic technical term for the whole body of practical commandments. Not listening to the divine voice is not an abstract theological failure but a behavioral deviation — Israel's feet went the wrong direction. The Torah given through Moses and the subsequent prophets is the road; Israel refused to walk it.
9.11: nitach aleinu ha'alah (nittaḥ ʿālênû hāʾālāh): 'The curse has been poured out upon us.' Alah (H423) is specifically the covenantal oath-curse — the treaty-curse that YHWH swore would come if Israel broke the covenant (Deut 28:15-68; Lev 26:14-43). The exile is the alah-sanction activated: not random misfortune but the precise covenantal consequence that Moses and the prophets had warned of. Nitach (poured out) — the same pouring-out language used for libation offerings is here applied to curse — the covenant has been poured out in judgment rather than blessing.
9.12: vayaqem et-devarav asher diber aleinu: 'He confirmed his words that he spoke against us.' YHWH's faithfulness works in two directions: he keeps his covenant promises of blessing (v.4), and he keeps his covenant threats of curse. The exile is proof of YHWH's consistency — his word is reliable even when it falls as judgment. This theological point is important: Israel cannot claim YHWH's promises while denying his warnings. Daniel's prayer acknowledges that YHWH acted justly in bringing the disaster — the prophets' warnings were true.
9.13: velo challinu et-penei YHWH Eloheinu lashuv meavoneinu: 'We did not entreat the face of YHWH our God, turning from our iniquities.' Challah et-panim (H2470 + H6440, entreat/pacify the face of) is the idiom for seeking divine favor through prayer and repentance. Lashuv (to return/repent): shuv (H7725) is the primary Hebrew repentance term — a turning-of-direction, not merely an internal feeling. The failure was not primarily intellectual (they did not understand the Torah rightly) but volitional: they knew but did not turn. Velo haskilnu be'amitecha (not attending to your truth) — emet as truth/faithfulness is the ground of prayer.
9.14: vayishkod YHWH al-hara'ah vayevi'iha aleinu: 'YHWH was watchful over the disaster and brought it upon us.' Shaqad (H8245, be watchful/alert) is the same root as the almond (shaqed) imagery in Jer 1:11-12 — YHWH is watching to perform his word. The disaster was not YHWH sleeping or ignoring; he was actively watching to execute his just judgment. Ki-tsaddiq YHWH Eloheinu al-kol-ma'asav asher asah ve'lo shamanu b'qolo: YHWH is righteous (tsaddiq) in all he has done — the exile vindicated rather than indicted YHWH's character.
9.15: ve'attah Adonai Eloheinu asher hotzeta et-amcha me'eretz Mitsraim beyad chazaqah: 'And now, Lord our God, who brought your people out of Egypt with a mighty hand.' The pivot from confession to petition is bridged by the Exodus appeal — YHWH's paradigmatic act of salvation. Yad chazaqah (mighty hand) is the standard Deuteronomic descriptor for the Exodus (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:8). Just as YHWH delivered from Egypt, so he can deliver from Babylon. The prayer's logic: the same God who acted then can act now, and his name's reputation is at stake in both cases.
9.16: bechol-tsidqotecha yashuv-na aphcha chamatha: 'In all your acts of righteousness, let your anger and wrath turn away.' Tsidqotecha (your righteousnesses): plural, referring to the accumulation of YHWH's saving acts in Israel's history — each rescue, each covenant renewal is an act of tsedaqah. These past acts are the basis for appealing to future action. The pairing of aph (burning anger) and chamah (wrath/heat) is the standard merism for divine anger — Daniel does not minimize the reality of YHWH's wrath but appeals for its redirection from the city to YHWH's mercy.
9.17: shema Elohenu el-tefillat avdecha: 'Hear, our God, the prayer of your servant.' Shema at the prayer's climax echoes the Shema of Deut 6:4 — the same verb now turned back to God. Daniel prays that YHWH will hear as he commanded Israel to hear. Veha'er panecha al-miqdashecha hashamem: 'Let your face shine upon your desolated sanctuary.' The sanctuary's desolation is both the problem and the theological test — a desolate temple suggests an absent God. The prayer is that YHWH's luminous presence will return to the destroyed place, reversing the departure of the glory described in Ezekiel 10-11.
9.18: ki lo al-tsidqoteinu anachnu maphilim tachanunenu lefanecha: 'For it is not on account of our righteousness that we present our pleas before you.' The final ground-clearing: all appeal to human merit is renounced. Tachanunenu (our pleas/supplications) — tachanun from chanan (H2603, show favor/grace) — the prayer itself is an appeal for grace, not a claim of righteousness. Ki al-rachamecha harabim: 'but on account of your great mercies.' The prayer rests entirely on the divine attribute of rachamim rabim (great mercies) — what YHWH is by nature and covenant, not what Israel has earned.
9.19: Adonai shema Adonai selach Adonai haqshivah va'aseh al-te'acher: 'Lord, hear! Lord, forgive! Lord, listen and act! Do not delay!' The triple invocation of Adonai creates an urgent litany — each address adds a new petition: hearing, forgiveness, attentive action, and urgency. Lemaan shimcha: 'for your own name's sake' — the prayer's ultimate appeal is YHWH's reputation among the nations. Ki shimcha nikra al-ircha ve'al-amecha: your name is called over your city and your people — the name-theology of covenant means YHWH's honor is bound up with the destiny of Jerusalem and Israel.
9.20: vod ani medabber umitpallel: 'While I was still speaking and praying.' The immediacy of the divine response (Gabriel arrives while Daniel is still speaking, v.21) demonstrates that YHWH hears prayer in real time. Matir chatati vachetat ami Yisrael: 'confessing my sin and the sin of my people Israel' — the intercessory prayer model is Daniel's own sin bound up with the community's. The individual representative mediates for the corporate body — the pattern of all biblical intercession.
9.21: vehaish Gavriel asher ra'iti bachazoni batchillah muaf biye'af: 'The man Gabriel whom I had seen in the vision at the beginning, flying swiftly.' Gavriel (H1403) = gavar (H1368, mighty/strong) + El (God) — the mighty one of God. This is one of only four named angels in the canonical OT/NT (Gabriel, Michael, Raphael in Tobit, and Lucifer/Satan as adversarial figure). Muaf biye'af: the phrase for flight is debated — either flying with swiftness or approaching in a state of exhaustion/weariness. Either way, Gabriel's arrival is direct divine response to Daniel's prayer.
9.22: vayiven oti vayedabber immi vayomar Daniel atah yatsati lehaskil binah: 'He made me understand and spoke to me, saying: Daniel, I have now come out to give you insight and understanding.' Lehaskil (to give insight/make wise) and binah (understanding): both from roots associated with wisdom and discernment. Gabriel's mission is not merely information delivery but cognitive enhancement — the revelation opens Daniel's understanding to grasp what mere human reasoning cannot reach. This is the model of biblical revelation: God gives not just data but the interpretive capacity to receive it.
9.23: bithchillat tachanuneicha yatsa dabar vaani bati lehagid ki chamudot attah: 'At the beginning of your pleas a word went out, and I have come to tell you, for you are greatly beloved.' Chamudot (H2532, beloved/precious/desirable): Daniel is described as ish chamudot (beloved man) in 10:11, 19 as well — a term of divine affection. The timing — the word went out at the beginning of the prayer — indicates that YHWH's response was initiated immediately upon Daniel's prayer, not after waiting for its completion. Intercessory prayer is heard as it begins.
9.25: umeshiach nagid shavuim shiva uveshavuim shishim ushnayim: 'And from the going out of the word to restore and build Jerusalem to the coming of an anointed prince, there shall be seven weeks. And for sixty-two weeks it shall be built again.' Mashiach nagid (anointed prince): mashiach is the anointed one — used for priests (Lev 4:3), kings (1 Sam 16:6; Ps 2:2), and here the expected eschatological figure. Nagid (H5057, prince/leader/commander) is a royal term. The mathematical structure (7 + 62 + 1 = 70 weeks) has generated centuries of calculation; the referents remain debated. The key theological point: YHWH has decreed a specific redemptive timeline, and the anointed one's coming is the center of the seventy-week period.
9.26: yikaret mashiach ve'ein lo (yikkārēt māšîaḥ wĕʾên lô): 'The anointed one will be cut off and will have nothing (or: not for himself).' Karet (H3772, cut off/cut down) is the standard Torah term for judicial death and covenant exclusion (used for capital offenses: Exod 31:14; Lev 7:20-21; Num 15:30-31). Ve'ein lo is ambiguous: either the anointed one receives nothing (no kingdom, no inheritance) or the cutting off is not for himself (i.e., not for his own sin — the same construction as Isa 53:8's mipesha ammi nega lamo). The latter reading aligns with Isaiah's Servant dying for others, not for his own transgressions.
9.27: vehigbir berit larabim shavua echad vachatsit hashavua yashbit zevach umincha: 'He shall make a strong covenant with many for one week, and for half of the week he shall put an end to sacrifice and offering.' Higbir berit (strengthen/confirm covenant): gavar (H1396, be strong/prevail) in Hiphil — to cause the covenant to prevail. Larabim (for the many) — the phrase echoes Isa 53:11-12 (yatsdiq tsaddiq avdi larabim, my righteous servant makes many righteous). Shiquts meshomem (abomination of desolation): shiquts (H8251, detestable thing/idol) + meshomem (H8074, causing desolation/stunned). This phrase (also 11:31; 12:11) becomes Jesus's own term for the end-time crisis (Mark 13:14 and parallels).

1.1: Third year of Jehoiakim — the dating creates a surface tension with Jer 25:1, which places Nebuchadnezzar's first western campaign in Jehoiakim's fourth year. The resolution lies in counting systems: Daniel follows Babylonian accession-year reckoning (the coronation year counts as year 0, so "year 1" begins at the first New Year after accession), while Jeremiah uses Judean non-accession reckoning (the coronation year itself is "year 1"). The two systems yield the same regnal year labelled differently by one integer. Nebukadretssar (H5019) is a Hebrew approximation of Akkadian Nabû-kudurri-uṣur ("Nebo protect the boundary-stone").
1.2: The Lord gave (Adonai, H136) — Daniel's narrator uses Adonai here rather than the covenant name YHWH. Daniel avoids YHWH throughout, reflecting the theological reticence of exile: the covenant Name belongs to the Land and Temple cult; in Babylon, the more universal titles Adonai and Elohim predominate. Elohim (H430) refers to both the temple of Israel's God and the house of "his god" (Marduk) in the same verse — a deliberate irony. Treasuries (otsar, H214) — the temple vessels ending in Babylon's treasury foreshadow the central crisis of ch 5.
1.3: Seed (zera, H2233) — a covenantal term normally used for offspring of the promise (Abraham, David, the holy seed). These young men are the covenant seed of Israel being absorbed into empire. Saris (H5631) may mean "eunuch" (literal) or simply "court official" (extended); the context suggests both roles existed, with the intent being social as well as physical reorientation.
1.4: Wisdom vocabulary triad: chokmah (H2451, practical wisdom), da'at (H1847, knowledge/relational knowing), and bin (H995, discernment/understanding) — the three epistemic terms of Proverbs' instruction. Babylon is consciously recruiting Israel's covenant-wisdom tradition for its own service. Ability (koach, H3581) implies physical and intellectual strength; these are Israel's finest, selected to represent the empire's cosmopolitan competence.
1.5: Daily portion (pathbag, H6598) — a Persian loanword, appearing only in Daniel and Esther in the Hebrew Bible, meaning the royal allotment of food and wine. The issue Daniel refuses is not merely dietary hygiene but covenantal boundary: food from the king's table was likely offered to Babylonian deities, making consumption an act of participation in Babylonian cult. Gadel (H1431, to rear/nourish) uses the same root as "great" — Babylon's program of greatness-formation is enacted through food. Three years — H7969 shaloshet; a fixed apprenticeship period, after which imperial service begins.
1.6: Four Hebrew theophoric names: Daniel (H1840, "El is my judge"), Hananiah (H2608, "YHWH has been gracious"), Mishael (H4332, "who is what El is?"), Azariah (H5838, "YHWH has helped"). All four embed Israel's covenant God; all four will be renamed. The tribe of Judah (H3063) is the messianic tribe — this detail is theologically loaded given later Davidic echoes.
1.7: Name replacement — the Babylonian names (Belteshazzar, Shadrach, Meshach, Abednego) embed Babylonian deity names (Bel, Aku/Marduk). The renaming is an act of identity colonization: to rename is to reclaim ownership, erasing the theophoric Hebrew names and their testimony to YHWH. The four men will demonstrate throughout the book that names do not determine identity.
1.8: Defile (ga'al, H1351) — to contaminate, make ritually impure. The same root underlies go'el (kinsman-redeemer) by ironic contrast: Daniel refuses what cannot be redeemed. Set upon his heart (sam al-libbo) — the lev (H3820) in Hebrew is the seat of decision and will, not merely sentiment. Daniel's act is a covenantal act of will, not just conscientious scruple. Sought permission (vayevaqesh, H1245) — the deliberate, courteous approach models how covenant faithfulness is exercised within empire: not provocative refusal but principled request.
1.9: Favour and compassion — chesed (H2617) and rachamim (H7356): the two great covenant-mercy terms appear together. God's response to Daniel's faithful resolve is not passive; God actively works within the bureaucratic structure of the empire to open the way. This pairing recurs in covenantal contexts throughout the OT (Exod 34:6; Hos 2:19).
1.10: Appearance (mar'eh, H4758) — physical countenance as visible evidence of inner state. The official's concern is practical: visible malnourishment would reflect badly on the palace and endanger him. Na'ar (H5288) = young man; the diminutive framing underlines their vulnerability within the imperial system.
1.11: The meltsar (H4453) — often translated "steward" or "overseer"; the term may be a loanword for a specific court functionary. Daniel goes to the more proximate official rather than forcing the issue with Ashpenaz, a pragmatic but also courteous strategy.
1.12: Vegetables (zeroa'im, H2235, lit. "things sown" or "seed-vegetables") — the word emphasizes plant-produce as opposed to meat. Water only (mayim, H4325) — the contrast with the wine of the king's table is sharp. The proposal is not asceticism but a practical boundary marker.
1.13: Compare (u're'eh, from H7200 ra'ah) — "and you will see"; an empirical test of whether faithfulness to covenant norms produces flourishing. The book consistently appeals to visible, verifiable outcomes as evidence of divine blessing.
1.14: He agreed (vayishma', from H8085 shama) — literally "he heard them." The same term used for obedience to God's voice is used here for the official's response to Daniel's request; hearing and heeding are both shama. The ten-day test period mirrors the ten plagues and other ten-day/ten-unit testing patterns in the OT.
1.15: Healthier and better nourished — bari (H1277, fat/robust) and besar (H1320, flesh); the physical evidence of covenantal blessing is unmistakable. "Better nourished" than those eating the royal menu subverts the implicit claim that imperial provision is the path to human flourishing.
1.16: The steward simply acts on what he observed: nasa' (H5375) their food and gave (natan, H5414) vegetables. The matter is resolved quietly and practically — no confrontation, no decree, just the empirical outcome vindicating Daniel's request.
1.17: Knowledge and wisdom given by God — the triad of ch 1:4 (chokmah, da'at, bin) is now attributed explicitly to divine gift, not Babylonian education. Chazon (H2377) and chalom (H2472) are distinguished: chazon is prophetic/visionary revelation (the major prophetic genre), while chalom is the ordinary dream vehicle. Daniel receives both. This verse is the theological hinge: whatever Babylon trained them in, God is the source of their extraordinary competence.
1.18: The miqetz (H7093, at the end of) — a typical Hebrew temporal formula marking the fulfillment of the appointed period. Hevisham (from H935, brought/presented) — the official presents them to Nebuchadnezzar for examination.
1.19: Stood before the king (vaya'amdu lifnei ha-melek, H5975) — to "stand before" a king is the technical phrase for entering royal service. None were found equal (nimtsa', H4672) — "to be found" is a legal/evaluative term; the four are the standard against which all others are measured.
1.20: Ten times (eser yadot) — hyperbolic superiority formula. Chartomim (H2748, magicians) and ashshaphim (H826, enchanters/astrologers) — the same two categories Pharaoh summons in Exod 7:11; the echo is not accidental. Daniel and his companions surpass Babylon's wise men as decisively as Moses surpassed Egypt's.
1.21: Daniel remained until the first year of Cyrus — a chronological bracket closing ch 1. The first year of Cyrus (H3566) = 539 BCE, when the Persians conquered Babylon and Cyrus issued his famous decree permitting exiles to return. Daniel's survival across the entire exile period — from the Babylonian conquest of Jerusalem to the Persian restoration — is itself a covenant-faithfulness witness.

2.1: Second year of Nebuchadnezzar — this appears to conflict with the three-year training in ch 1, but the Babylonian accession-year system again resolves it: the "second year" from Nebuchadnezzar's accession falls within the training period if it began after the initial capture. Ruach (H7307) = spirit/breath; Nebuchadnezzar's ruach was agitated — the same term used for the Spirit of God moving over the waters (Gen 1:2). Shena (H8142, sleep) fled from him; divine unrest preparing for revelation.
2.2: Four categories of Babylonian specialists summoned: chartomim (H2748, magicians), ashshaphim (H826, enchanters), mekashephim (H3784, sorcerers), and kasdim (H3778, Chaldeans — here a professional class of diviners, not merely an ethnic designation). This fourfold list mirrors Exod 7:11 (Pharaoh's magicians), placing Nebuchadnezzar's wisdom establishment in the typological line of every empire that opposes the purposes of the God of Israel.
2.3: Saam (H5590) — his spirit was troubled; the same root appears in Gen 41:8 when Pharaoh's spirit was troubled after his dream. The structural parallel with Joseph is deliberate: a Hebrew in a foreign court, given understanding of a king's dream, rises to power. Daniel surpasses Joseph as the pattern escalates.
2.4: In Aramaic (Aramit, H762) — the book's language shifts here from Hebrew to Biblical Aramaic and does not return to Hebrew until 8:1. Aramaic was the lingua franca of the ancient Near East from the 8th century BCE; the shift signals the international scope of what follows — the Aramaic section (2:4b–7:28) addresses the nations as such. Chelem (Aramaic H2493, dream) replaces the Hebrew chalom. Peshar (H6591, interpretation) — this Aramaic term becomes the technical vocabulary of apocalyptic disclosure.
2.5: The matter is gone from me (milleta min-ni azdat) — azda (H230) means certain/decreed; the king's decree is fixed. If they cannot provide both dream and interpretation, the consequence is death and the destruction of their houses. The impossible demand reveals that the Babylonian wise men's entire system depends on the king supplying the dream — they can interpret but not reveal. Only God can do both.
2.6: Peshar recurs; the conditional promise of gifts and honour (yeqar, H3367, honour/dignity; segu, H7690, abundant gifts) frames the test as both threat and reward. The term yeqar will reappear in Nebuchadnezzar's ultimate doxology (v.37).
2.7: The wise men repeat their request for the dream (chelem) first, then interpretation (peshar) — they need the raw material before they can function. Their system is one of interpretive pattern-matching applied to known data; revelation of the unknown is beyond them.
2.8: Yatzib (H3330, certain/fixed) — the king perceived they were buying time (zaban, H2118, to gain time). The confrontation exposes the fundamental limitation of human wisdom: it can manage what is already known but cannot access the divine council.
2.9: The king's accusation is essentially correct: without the ability to tell the dream, any interpretation could be fabricated. The demand for the dream itself is a kind of epistemological test — only genuine access to the divine could produce it.
2.10: There is not a man on earth — enash (H606, Aramaic for "man/person") — the universal negative claim of the wise men is theologically correct even if they do not intend it that way: no human being has access to the king's dream. Only "the gods whose dwelling is not with flesh" (beshar, H1321) — they inadvertently articulate the gap that only Daniel's God can bridge.
2.11: A rare thing (yaqqir, H3358) — difficult/precious/rare. The gods do not dwell with flesh (beshar): the Babylonian theologians acknowledge that divine-human communication is exceptional. Daniel's God closes this gap precisely because he is the God who reveals mysteries — the same God who became flesh (John 1:14) will later be described as one who "dwells with" his people (Rev 21:3).
2.12: The decree to destroy all wise men of Babylon would include Daniel and his companions — they are caught in the crisis they did not cause. Beaz (H1149, in anger) — Nebuchadnezzar's rage is the context for Daniel's intervention.
2.13: Daniel and his companions were sought (mitbaqqurin, from H1156, to seek) for execution. The situation is urgent. The verb is the same as be'a (to pray/petition) in v.18 — the seeking that threatens their lives will be matched by the seeking that saves them.
2.14: Daniel speaks with ta'em u-ve'em (H2942, prudence and discretion) — ta'em is often used for discernment/judgment; Daniel does not panic but responds with the wisdom God has already given him (1:17). He asks for time — zeman (H2166, appointed time) — the same term used for the eschatological "time" throughout Daniel.
2.15: The question "why is the decree so urgent?" (me-di milleta machtsepah min-qadam malka) — machtsepah (H2685, urgent/pressing). Daniel seeks information before acting — the wisdom response to crisis is to understand before reacting.
2.16: U-ve'ah (H1156, petitioned/requested) — Daniel requests time from the king, and the king grants it (shim, H7761, appointed/set). This brief verse is structurally important: Daniel does not pray first then act; he takes the practical step with the king first, then brings his companions into prayer. Wisdom and prayer are integrated, not sequential alternatives.
2.17: Chavrayhi (H2269, companions/friends) — Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah are Daniel's covenant community within exile. He does not face the crisis alone; the corporate nature of the response reflects the communal character of prayer in Israel.
2.18: Seek mercy (le-mevey rachamim) — rachamim (H7356) again (cf. 1:9); God's tender mercy is the constant resource. Mystery (raz, H7328) — a Persian loanword meaning "secret" or "hidden thing," here entering Daniel's theological vocabulary for the first time. Raz will appear nine times in Daniel (chs. 2 and 4), always for the divine secret that only God can disclose. The NT inherits this vocabulary: Paul's mystērion (mystery of Christ, Eph 3:3-4) is the Greek equivalent of raz.
2.19: Night vision (chezu leilya) — chezu (H2376, Aramaic for vision) + leilya (H3916, at night). The raz was revealed (gali, H1541, to reveal/uncover) to Daniel — the same verb used for the revealing of the covenant (galah) throughout the OT. Daniel blesses (berakhi, H1289) the God of heaven immediately — praise precedes petition.
2.20: Daniel's doxology begins: leheve shemeh di-Elaha mevarekh ("blessed be the name of God") — shemeh (H8036, name) + mevarekh (H1289, blessed). Chokmah (H2452, wisdom) and geburah (H1370, power/strength) belong to God from eternity (min-alam ve-ad alam, H5957, from ages to ages). The doxological form mirrors 1 Chr 29:11-12 (David's prayer) and anticipates Rev 5:12 ("worthy is the Lamb to receive power and wisdom").
2.21: He changes times and seasons (idanaya u-zimnaya) — idan (H5732) and zeman (H2166) both mean appointed time/season; the pairing is emphatic. God removes and establishes kings (melkin, H4430): the political vision of Daniel grounds royal power entirely in divine appointment — Nebuchadnezzar's kingdom is gift and loan, not achievement. Gives wisdom to the wise — the paradox of grace: wisdom is given to those already counted wise, deepening what they have received.
2.22: Reveals deep and hidden things (gali atikin u-mesattarin) — atiq (H6260, deep/ancient) and setir (H5642, hidden/concealed). The contrast between divine knowing and darkness (cheshokha, H2816) and light (nehora, H5094) is cosmological. God dwells in light and knows what is in the darkness — anticipating later apocalyptic passages where the heavenly throne shines like fire (7:9).
2.23: Daniel addresses God as "the God of my fathers" (Elah avahathi) — the covenant formula, invoking patriarchal continuity. Hoda (H3029, I give thanks/praise) + meshabbach (H7624, I praise). The shift from doxology (vv.20-22) to personal gratitude (v.23) marks the movement from cosmic to relational: the same God who sets kings over kingdoms has now answered Daniel's personal prayer.
2.24: Daniel does not rush to the king directly but first returns to Arioch to spare the wise men. Al-te'abed (do not destroy, H7), the first word of the verse, is a plea for life — Daniel's concern for the other wise men even in his crisis moment reflects the moral character of covenant wisdom.
2.25: Behitvehal (H927, with haste) — Arioch brings Daniel hurriedly. The framing of Daniel as the one "who can make known the interpretation" (peshar) sets up the theological claim: only one who stands in the divine council can bridge the gap the wise men declared unbridgeable (v.10-11).
2.26: The king's question: "are you able to make known to me the dream and its interpretation?" — khayel (H3546, are you able) echoes the wise men's "is there any man who yikhel?" (v.10). The answer will be: not Daniel, but Daniel's God.
2.27: Daniel's response begins with the explicit disclaimer: no wise man (chakimin, H2445), enchanter (ashshaphim, H826), magician (chartumim, H2749), or diviner (gazerin, H1505) can reveal this — the full fourfold list from v.2 is negated. The critique is systemic: Babylonian wisdom as an institution cannot access the divine council.
2.28: There is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries — the thesis of the chapter and one of Daniel's central theological claims. Elah bishmayya (H426 + H8065, the God in heaven) — transcendent, unlike the gods who cannot dwell with flesh (v.11). Raz (mystery) + moda (H3046, he makes known) — God's response to human epistemic limitation is not silence but revelation. What will be in the latter days (acharit yomayya, H320 + H3118) — the eschatological formula; literally "the end of days," the technical phrase for the consummation of God's purposes.
2.29: The mysteries revealed correspond to the king's own nocturnal pondering (ra'yonayka, H7476, thoughts/concerns on your bed). The dream is not random but responsive: God meets the king's genuine question about the future with a genuine answer, however unwelcome the answer may prove.
2.30: La di-chokmah di-itai vi ana min-kol-chayaya — "not because of any wisdom that is in me more than all living." Daniel explicitly disclaims any personal superiority: the revelation is given for the king's sake (di-peshar le-malka yehavon) and for the sake of understanding (ve-rayyoney libbak tinda). Humility before revelation is a consistent posture throughout the book.
2.31: Image (tsalma, H6755) — the Aramaic cognate of Hebrew tselem (image), the word used in Gen 1:26-27 for humanity as the image of God. The great image (H7690 saggiy, great/mighty) is the antithesis of the human image: where humanity bears the image of the living God, this image of human imperial glory will be shattered by the stone. The statue's ziw (H2122, brightness/brilliance) was terrifying — the language of divine theophany applied to human empire.
2.32: The metals descend in value but increase in hardness (gold → silver → bronze → iron), while the final material (iron mixed with clay, v.33) paradoxically combines hardness with brittleness. This inverse relationship — decreasing glory but increasing political hardness, ultimately terminating in a brittle mixture — is the interpretive key to the four-kingdom sequence.
2.33: Parzel (H6523, iron) and chaser (H2635, clay) — the mixture of two incompatible materials. Iron does not bond with clay; the kingdom it represents will have apparent strength but structural fragility at every joint.
2.34: Stone cut without hands (eben di-gizrat la vi-yadayin) — eben (H69) + gizrat (H1505, cut/carved) + la vi-yadayin (not by hands). The passive divine agency — the stone's origin is supernatural, not human political process. Macha (H4223, struck/smote) — the stone strikes the feet of the statue; the kingdom of God begins not at the top but at the foundational weakness of the structure.
2.35: The shattering produces material that becomes "like the chaff of the summer threshing floors" (kemotz di-idaran qayt) — the same image as Ps 1:4 and Isa 17:13 for what is blown away in judgment. The stone then fills the whole earth — a mountain (tur, H2906) encompassing everything, the inverse of the localized and decomposing statue. The imagery is total displacement, not coexistence.
2.36: Denah chelmah (this is the dream) — the dream is declared as completed narration; now the peshar (interpretation) follows. The transition is formally marked, emphasizing that the revelation and its interpretation are two distinct gifts, both from God.
2.37: King of kings (malak malkin) — an Akkadian/Aramaic royal title, used for Nebuchadnezzar by a Jewish exile deliberately: the king who thinks himself absolute sovereign is addressed with the imperial title, but immediately subordinated — "the God of heaven has given you the kingdom." Malkhuta (H4437, kingdom), chaylah (H2429, power), taqpah (H8632, strength), yeqara (H3367, honour) — the four attributes of the kingdom are given, not inherent.
2.38: Nebuchadnezzar is the golden head — the greatest of the four kingdoms. The dominion over "wherever the children of man dwell, the beasts of the field and the birds of the air" deliberately echoes Gen 1:28 (human dominion over creation), but here it is granted to a pagan king by the same God who granted dominion to Adam — the dominion mandate operating through an imperial vessel.
2.39: The second kingdom (achori malkhuta, a kingdom inferior to yours) and the third (bronze, ruling over all the earth) — the identifications of Medo-Persia and Greece are historically robust readings, though the text does not name them. The interpretive move from gold to silver to bronze enacts the deterioration: each kingdom receives less of the explicit divine endorsement given to Nebuchadnezzar.
2.40: Fourth kingdom, strong as iron — parzel di-mara kulla (iron that breaks everything). The Roman Empire reading (traditional) fits the political history; the point is structural: the kingdom with the greatest apparent force also carries within it the fatal mixture. Teqoph (H8632, strength) appears again here — the iron's strength is real but the brittleness of v.41-43 qualifies it fundamentally.
2.41: Feet partly of iron and partly of clay (pesig di-parzel u-ve-chaser) — pesig (H6536, divided) signals fracture built into the structure. The mixture of iron and clay is specifically described as "mixed human seed" (zera enasha, H2234 + H606) — possibly intermarriage between different peoples or fusion of incompatible elements within an empire. The strength is real but the binding is false.
2.42: Ve-etzbeata di-reglayyah (the toes of the feet) — the toes are specified as partly strong, partly broken. The image builds: the statue is internally divided from its base, combining force and fragility in the same material and the same governance structure.
2.43: They will mingle (mitharevin, H6151) but will not hold together (la yidbequn da-im da) — dbeq (H1693) is the adhesion/cleave verb, the same word used for covenant bonding in Gen 2:24 (a man cleaves to his wife). What cannot cleave by covenant nature cannot hold.
2.44: God of heaven will establish a kingdom — yaqim Elah bishmayya malkhuta. This kingdom has four negatives: it will never be destroyed (la tithavad), never surrendered to another people (la tishtebiq le-am achoran), it will crush (tediqq) and bring to an end all those kingdoms (kol-illen malkhwata), and it will endure forever (hi tequm le-alam). The divine kingdom is defined entirely by what it is not subject to — decay, transfer, defeat, termination.
2.45: Stone cut from the mountain without hands — the interpretation confirms the image: the stone cut without human agency represents the kingdom the God of heaven establishes without human political process. Uman (H540, made sure/certain) — the same root as the Hebrew amen: the dream is certain (aman), the interpretation trustworthy. The epistemological confidence of prophecy rests in the character of the One who reveals.
2.46: Nebuchadnezzar fell on his face (nifal al-anpuhi) — prostration before Daniel. Segid (H5457, to bow/worship) — the same Aramaic term that will be demanded of everyone before the gold image in ch 3, making Nebuchadnezzar's act here a pivot: the king who demands universal worship is himself on his face before a Jewish exile who represents the God who reveals. Minchah (H4504, offering) and nichoach (H5208, pleasing/fragrant) — sacrificial vocabulary applied to Daniel, a gesture that over-reads Daniel's role even if well-intentioned.
2.47: Your God is God of gods and Lord of kings — Elah elahin u-mare malkin. The king's confession is formally correct: the God of Israel is supreme over other gods and over all earthly rulers. Revealer of mysteries (galah razin) — the compound title summarizes the entire chapter's theological argument. Nebuchadnezzar's confession is real but temporary (cf. ch 4); it is not yet the total submission of heart that the book awaits.
2.48: Nefaqu saggiye (great gifts) and shalit rav (chief governor) — Daniel's elevation recapitulates the Joseph pattern (Gen 41:39-44). Shiltan (H7984, authority/dominion) over the province of Babylon and chief (rav, H7229) over all the wise men — the Jewish exile administers Babylonian wisdom, the reversal of the acculturation program of ch 1.
2.49: Daniel's intercession for his three companions — u-Daniel ba'ah min-qadam malka (Daniel petitioned from before the king). The pattern of ch 1 repeats at the apex of ch 2: Daniel uses his position to secure the welfare of his covenant community. He is at court (ve-Daniel bi-sha'ar malka, at the king's gate — the location of civic authority), exercising imperial power in service of covenant faithfulness.

12.1: Michael (Mikha'el, H4317, "who is like God?") — the name is a rhetorical question asserting divine incomparability; in Daniel Michael functions as sar (H8269, prince/guardian) specifically over Israel (10:21). His "arising" (ya'amod, H5975) echoes the same verb used for Daniel's own promised resurrection in v.13 — the angelic champion stands, and so will Daniel. Time of distress (et tsarah, H6256 + H6869) — the temporal phrase uses tsarah gedolah (great trouble/tribulation), an intensification of the exodus vocabulary (Deut 4:30, ba-tsarah lekha). "Such as never was since there was a nation" — the incomparable-distress formula echoes Joel 2:2 and anticipates its NT application (Matt 24:21). Book (sefer, H5612) — the book of the living, in which Israel's rescued are enrolled; cf. Exod 32:32-33, Ps 69:28, Mal 3:16.
12.2: Many who sleep in the dust — rabbim miyshene admat-afar: rabbim (H7227, many) is the same word used in Isa 53:11 (the Servant justifying rabbim); yashen (H3463, sleep) as a euphemism for death; afar (H6083, dust) — the ground into which Adam was returned (Gen 3:19). This verse directly reverses the death sentence of Genesis 3. Everlasting life (chayyei olam, H2416 + H5769) — the phrase appears here for the first time in the OT in this exact form; Jesus takes it directly into Greek as zōē aiōnios (John 3:16; 5:24). Contempt (dera'on, H1860) — this rare noun appears only here and Isa 66:24 ("the worm does not die"), making the book of Daniel close on a conscious echo of Isaiah's closing vision of final judgment. The two destinations — chayyei olam and dera'on — are the sharpest OT formulation of eternal division.
12.3: The wise will shine (maskilim yaz'hiru) — maskil (H7919, wise/instructed) is also the hiphil participle of H2094 zahar, meaning "to cause to shine/warn/teach"; the verbal range is deliberate: those who have been enlightened and enlighten others shine with reflected glory. Brightness of the firmament (zohar ha-raqia') — zohar (H2096, brightness) + raqia' (H7549, the firmament of Gen 1:6-8): the vocabulary of creation's luminaries is applied to the resurrection glory of the faithful. Like the stars forever (kochavim le-olam va-ed) — Paul draws on this tradition in 1 Cor 15:41-44 (the resurrection body compared to different degrees of stellar glory). The stars language echoes Abraham's descendants (Gen 15:5; 22:17) and Daniel's own vision of the holy ones as heavenly host.
12.4: Shut up and seal the words (setom vachatom ha-devarim) — two near-synonyms: satam (H5640, to stop up/close) and chatam (H2856, to seal with a signet). Sealing a scroll preserves its contents for the appointed time; this is not suppression but eschatological safekeeping (cf. Rev 5:1 where the sealed scroll awaits the Lamb). Until the time of the end (le-et qetz, H6256 + H7093) — qetz appears in Daniel as the decisive technical term for the eschatological terminus; it first entered Daniel's vocabulary at 8:17 and now closes the book. Knowledge will increase (tarbeh ha-da'at, H7235 + H1847) — da'at is relational/covenantal knowledge, not merely information; the increase corresponds to the unsealing of the scroll, not technological progress.
12.5: Two angelic figures on the river banks — the scene echoes the theophany of ch 10 (the "man clothed in linen" over the river, H2975 ye'or). The doubling of witnesses (shenayim acherim, two others) recalls the legal requirement of two witnesses (Deut 19:15); the revelation of the final things is confirmed by heavenly testimony. The river (ye'or, the Nile/great river) grounds the vision in the liminal space between the earthly and heavenly.
12.6: How long? — ad-matai (H4970), the prophetic question asked also in Pss 13, 74, 79, 89, and Hab 1:2. The question is addressed to "the man clothed in linen above the waters" (levush ha-badim, H906 bad — fine linen, the priestly material; cf. Lev 16:4, Ezek 9:2-3). The figure's position above the waters inverts the standing position of the two figures on the banks — elevation signals authority.
12.7: Swore by him who lives forever (vayishava be-chai ha-olam) — the oath by "the living forever one" (H2416 + H5769) is the strongest possible guarantee; in the OT only God swears by himself (Gen 22:16; Isa 45:23). Time, times, and half a time (le-moed moedim va-chatsi, H4150 + H5732 + H2677) — the three-and-a-half period recurs from 7:25; using moed (appointed time, festival-time) rather than the Aramaic idan, the Hebrew text loads the interval with covenantal calendar resonance. Shattering the power of the holy people (kenaphets yad am-qadosh) — naphets (H5310, shatter/scatter); yad (H3027, hand/power) — when the power of God's people has been utterly broken, then (ve-khallenah, H3615, then it will be finished) all these things will be completed.
12.8: Daniel's admission: shama'ti ve-lo avin — "I heard but I did not understand." Shama' (H8085) normally implies comprehension; the qualification "but did not understand" (bin, H995) creates an instructive gap: the revelation is genuine and recorded, but its full significance remains sealed. The prophet can be a faithful transmitter without being a complete interpreter. Acharit ella (H319, the outcome/end of these things) — Daniel asks for closure; he receives instead a command to proceed without it.
12.9: Go your way (lekh Daniel) — a gentle dismissal of the question. The words are setumim ve-chatumin (shut up and sealed, from the same roots as v.4) — the doubling is intentional: the sealing is confirmed even after Daniel's question. Until the time of the end recurs; the structure of non-understanding is constitutive for the reader also — the book closes on intentional opacity that models the eschatological posture of waiting trust.
12.10: Purified, made white, refined — three metallurgical/laundry terms: yitbararu (H1305, barar — to cleanse/purify, used for sifting grain and refining metal), yitlabenu (H3835, laban — to make white, used for bleaching linen), and yitsarafu (H6884, tsaraph — to smelt/refine precious metal). The triple refinement language is used for Mal 3:2-3 (the refiner's fire) and Zech 13:9. The wicked will continue to act wickedly (hirshi'u resha'im, H7561 + H7563) — the hardening effect of tribulation: the same period purifies the wise and hardens the wicked. Maskilim will understand — the verb yavinu (H995) is the same as Daniel's own non-understanding in v.8; but in the time of the end the maskilim will have the understanding Daniel was denied.
12.11: Daily sacrifice abolished (hussar ha-tamid, H8548 + H5493) — tamid (the perpetual/daily offering; Num 28:3-8) has been the measure of regular covenant worship; its removal marks the beginning of the crisis period. Abomination of desolation (shiqquts meshomem, H8251 + H8074) — shiqquts (detestable/abominable thing) is the Hebrew term for idols (cf. 1 Kgs 11:5, 7); shomem means desolating/laying waste. This phrase is picked up explicitly by Jesus (Matt 24:15, "the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel"). 1,290 days — 30 days longer than the 1,260 days (42 months) of 7:25; the relationship between the time intervals is interpretively open.
12.12: Blessed is the one who waits (ashrei ha-mechakkeh, H835 + H2442) — the beatitude form (ashrei from Ps 1:1) appears here for the one who patiently endures to the end. Chikah (H2442, to wait/tarry) is the active posture of faith under pressure; the same verb used in Isa 8:17 ("I will wait for YHWH") and Hab 2:3 ("wait for it; it will certainly come"). 1,335 days — 45 days beyond the 1,290; the arithmetic difference points to an additional period of blessing beyond the crisis's end, but the text does not specify its content.
12.13: Go your way till the end (ve-atah lekh le-qetz) — the final command given twice (cf. v.9). Daniel is to live out his remaining days in faithful service without requiring further explanation. You will rest (tanuach, H5117) — nuach is the root of Noah's name and the Sabbath rest; Daniel's death is framed as covenant Sabbath-rest. Stand in your lot at the end of days (ta'amod le-goralekha le-qetz ha-yamim) — amad (H5975, to stand) is the resurrection verb from v.1 (Michael "stood"); goral (H1486, lot/allotment) is the Joshua land-portion vocabulary (Josh 14:2; 15:1) — the inheritance of the Land is now the eschatological frame for individual resurrection. Daniel will receive his allotted portion in the covenant inheritance. The book closes not on a cosmic calendar but on a personal resurrection promise.

3.1: tselem (ṣelem, Aramaic): image, statue. The same word used in Dan 2:31 for the statue in Nebuchadnezzar's dream — whose head was gold. Here Nebuchadnezzar makes an entire image of gold, apparently extending the gold-head glory to the whole statue. The dimension ratio (60 cubits tall, 6 cubits wide — roughly 90 × 9 feet) is unusually narrow; some commentators suggest a statue on a pedestal. The plain of Dura (biqdath dura, the plain of Dura) is unidentified but the name suggests a walled enclosure. The Babylonian setting is historically plausible: large royal cultic images are attested in Neo-Babylonian texts.
3.2: rab-ravravan (rab-rabrĕwān): an honorific superlative — the most exalted leaders. The list in vv. 2-3 includes seven categories of Babylonian court officials (satraps, prefects, governors, counselors, treasurers, justices, magistrates). The sevenfold list is comprehensive — the whole administrative hierarchy is summoned. The political intent is total: Nebuchadnezzar wants universal imperial compliance with the dedication.
3.3: The repetition of the full official list from v. 2 in v. 3 is a deliberate literary device — the narrator emphasizes both the command and its compliance with the same catalogue. The phrase qamah letsel tsalma (qāmāh leṣēl ṣalmāʾ: stood before the image) uses the spatial verb qum (stand/arise) — they have physically placed themselves in the posture of deference before the image. The repetition creates the impression of mass, relentless pressure.
3.4: qara (qārēʾ): the herald cried out. The Aramaic karoz (herald, v. 4) is a loanword from Persian kāruza or Greek kēryx — one of Daniel's Aramaic Persian/Greek loanwords that scholars note. The announcement formula (lekhon amreen ammin umevaya uleshanaya: “to you it is commanded, peoples and nations and languages”) uses the triadic nations-formula that appears throughout the book (2:44; 3:4; 4:1; 5:19; 6:25; 7:14) — every empire-spanning utterance in Daniel reaches all peoples, nations, and languages, the same universal formula the NT applies to Christ's dominion (Rev 5:9; 7:9).
3.5: nefila' (nip̄lûn, imperfect): you will fall and worship. The imperfect in Aramaic carries imperatival force here (a future-as-command). sagad (sĕgid): to prostrate oneself in worship — the technical term for cult-prostration throughout ch. 3, occurring eleven times in the chapter. The instruments listed (qarna, mashroqita, qathros, sabbeka, psanterin, sumponeyya) include a mix of Aramaic/Semitic and Greek loanwords (qathros = Greek kithara, harp; psanterin = Greek psalterion; sumponeyya = Greek symphōnia, bagpipes or harmony) — evidence of the Persian-Greek cultural milieu, exactly what would be expected in a sixth-fifth century Babylonian court.
3.6: atun nura (attûn nûrāʾ): furnace of fire. The atun is a large smelting/burning furnace, likely a brick kiln similar to those used for the massive Babylonian building projects. The phrase yaqidta (burning hot) emphasizes intensity. The death penalty as immediate and public — without trial — is the political function of a cultic test: refusal is treated as sedition, not just impiety. The furnace as execution device is attested in ancient Near Eastern legal contexts (Jer 29:22 refers to Zedekiah's officials being roasted by Nebuchadnezzar).
3.7: The response of the entire assembly: sagad — they prostrated themselves and worshipped the image. The Aramaic construction nefila sagdeen (falling, worshipping) uses two simultaneous participles describing a single act: the physical prostration is the worship. The mass compliance of “all the peoples, nations, and languages” at the sound of music underscores the social pressure facing the three men — the entire world system bows. The counter-narrative of three men standing is Daniel's emphasis: faithful resistance against universal pressure.
3.8: qeretsayyata (qĕreṣayyāʾ): they accused, or literally “ate the pieces of” — an idiom for slander/denunciation derived from the image of a predator consuming. The Chaldeans (kasday) are Babylon's elite priestly-scholarly class; their denunciation of the Jews is both political (they resent the Jews' elevation, cf. 2:49) and religious (they are the guardians of Babylonian cult). The phrase kasday qeretsayyata yehudaya (Chaldeans accused Jews) sets up the ethnic and religious conflict explicitly.
3.9: malka lealam kheyey (malkāʾ lĕʿālam ḥĕyay): O king, live forever. This is the standard Babylonian/Persian court greeting formula. In Aramaic it uses the jussive-imperative: “may you live forever.” Its repetition at every royal appearance in Daniel (2:4; 3:9; 5:10; 6:6, 21) functions as a literary marker of the human court's pretension to divine permanence — a pretension YHWH consistently exposes in the book.
3.10: The accusers restate the king's decree verbatim: this is the narrative technique of exact citation to establish the legal precision of the charge. The decree is unambiguous — no escape clause for conscience or religion. The literary function is to make the three men's refusal fully deliberate: they know exactly what they are refusing. The precision of the charge also underscores the clarity of YHWH's vindication: if the accusation is exact, the deliverance is equally exact.
3.11: man-hu alaha (man hûʾ ʾĕlāhāʾ): who is the god? — the rhetorical question embedded in the accusers' speech (if they do not bow, they are to be thrown into the furnace) sets the stage for the king's own version of the same question in v. 15. The Aramaic question-form with the emphatic man (who?) is used throughout Daniel for challenges to divine authority.
3.12: la-ilu elahach pelachain (lāʾ-ilāhāk pāleḥîn): they are not serving your gods. The verb pelach is the Aramaic term for religious service/cult-service — used for both pagan worship and YHWH-service. The accusation is precisely the violation of cultic loyalty: the three men serve a different lord. The names Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego (shadrak, meshak, aved-nego) are Babylonian court names given in 1:7 to replace their Hebrew names — but their Hebrew loyalty persists behind the Babylonian veneer.
3.13: ach birgaz u-chemah (ʾeḏayin nebukhad-netstsar birgaz ûḥĕmāʾ): then Nebuchadnezzar in rage and fury. The two synonyms for anger (rigza = rage/trembling, chema = hot anger/wrath) appear together for emphasis — the king's emotion is intense. Aramaic frequently uses paired synonyms for emphasis, particularly in court-speech. The repetition of his full name in the narrative context is also emphatic: the narrator does not abbreviate to “the king.”
3.14: hatsteva (haṣṣĕḇāʾ): Is it true? Or intentional? The Aramaic word can mean “deliberate/on purpose.” The king's question implies disbelief — he promoted these men (1:7; 2:49), and their defiance is personally offensive. The question form (is it true) before pronouncing judgment shows a vestige of due process, though the outcome is pre-decided by the rage of vv. 13, 19.
3.15: man-hu alaha di yesheyzevinnkon mi-yadi (man hûʾ ʾĕlāhāʾ dî yĕšêzĕvinnĕkôn mîyadî): who is the god who will deliver you from my hand? This is the theological climax of the confrontation: Nebuchadnezzar's question is a direct challenge to YHWH's existence and power. The phrase mi-yadi (from my hand) echoes the Exodus language where Pharaoh asks “who is YHWH that I should obey his voice?” (Exod 5:2). The answer to both pharaoh and Nebuchadnezzar is the same: YHWH delivers his people from the hands of the most powerful rulers on earth.
3.16: la khashcheen anan lehotava-k pethgam al-dena (lāʾ-ḥaššĕḥîn ʾănaḥnāʾ lĕhātābāʾk pittĕgām ʿal-dĕnāh): we have no need to answer you in this matter. The three men's response is remarkable in its confidence: la khashchin = we are not compelled/anxious. The root khash suggests urgency or need — they feel no urgency to defend themselves. The answer is not defiance but trust: the issue does not require their advocacy because YHWH is the one who will speak through the outcome.
3.17: ithai elahana di anan palekheen yakhil leshezavah min-atun nura yaqidta (ʾîṯay ʾĕlāhana dî ʾănaḥnāʾ pālĕḥîn yākil lĕšêzābāh min-ʾattûn nûrāʾ yāqiḏtāʾ): if our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace. The Aramaic conditional hn ... ithai (if there is) does not express doubt but marks the condition rhetorically; the real statement is the conviction of v. 18. yakhil (yākil): is able — the same root as the NT Greek dunamai. The ability of YHWH is affirmed even while acknowledging that deliverance is not guaranteed on human terms.
3.18: v'hen la yedaʿ leh malka di le-ilahacha la felechin vli-tselem dahava la sagdeen (wĕhin lāʾ yĕḏaʿ leh malkāʾ dî lēʾ-ʾĕlāhāk lāʾ pāleḥîn): and if not — be it known to you, O king, that we will not serve your gods or worship the golden image. The unconditional confession: v'hen la (and if not) — even if YHWH does not deliver us, we will not bow. This is the pivot of the entire narrative: the faith statement that does not make deliverance contingent on rescue. The theological depth is enormous: the three men trust YHWH not because he will certainly rescue them but because he is YHWH. This is the same unconditional faith as Job 13:15 (though he slay me, yet will I trust in him).
3.19: hash-tannei tsvey anpoh (ḥaš-tannê ṣĕwê ʾanpôhi): the form of his face was changed — literally changed the appearance of his face. The Aramaic shannei means to change/alter; the noun tsevah (form/color) is related to the word for color or appearance. The furnace temperature is increased sevenfold — a hyperbolic number expressing extreme intensification. The literary structure mirrors the defiance: the three men refused to bend; the king bends the furnace temperature. Human extremity only sets up divine demonstration.
3.20: gibbare chayil (gibbārê ḥayil): mighty men of valor. The superlative construction uses the noun gibbar (warrior, mighty one) with chayil (strength, wealth, army) — the most elite soldiers available. That these are the very men who are killed by the flame while throwing the three into the furnace (v. 22) is an ironic reversal: human power is destroyed by its own instrument of execution; those it kills are unharmed.
3.21: Bound in their clothing: sarbalehon patishehon vekarbaltehon — the list of garments includes several Persian loanwords (sarbel = tunic, patish = possibly a garment or mantle, karbela = perhaps a cap or head covering). The detail that they were thrown in fully clothed — no stripping, no preparation — makes the subsequent observation (“not even their clothing smelled of fire,” v. 27) more remarkable. The binding (kefitu, bound/tied) emphasizes helplessness: they are thrown helpless and fully clothed into the furnace.
3.22: gevurta malka yaqidat (giḇûrtāʾ malkāʾ yaqiḏat): the king's command was urgent/fierce. The root geburat (might, fierceness) is related to gibbar (warrior) — the same word used for the mighty men of v. 20. The irony is complete: the king's mighty command kills his own mighty men while the unarmed prisoners walk free. The causal structure — because the king's command was fierce — is the narrator's way of saying that human extremity of power is self-destructive.
3.23: The three men fell bound into the midst of the burning furnace. The Aramaic naflu (they fell) is the same verb root as the “falling down to worship” demanded of all peoples (vv. 5-7). The three men who refused to fall in worship of the image are now literally fallen into the furnace — the irony of position. They fall not in worship but in faith, into the fire that will not harm them.
3.24: etdahar malka (ʾeṯdahar malkāʾ): the king was alarmed, startled. The root dahar means to terrify, to disturb greatly. The king arose in haste (behitbehala) — the same root as the “haste” of frightened men throughout Daniel (4:5; 5:9). His question — “did we not throw three men bound into the fire?” — is forensically careful before he announces the vision of four men walking in the fire.
3.25: bar-elaheen (bar-ʾĕlāhîn): son of the gods. The plural elaheen (gods) reflects Nebuchadnezzar's polytheistic frame: he sees the fourth figure as a divine being, one of the elaheen. The singular bar (son of) individualizes the being: not a generic divine, but one specific divine figure. Christian readers have traditionally seen a Christophany here — the pre-incarnate Son appearing in the fire with his servants. The ambiguity between ben-elahim (son of God) and bar-elaheen (son of the gods) is theologically rich: Nebuchadnezzar can only name what he sees in his frame; the narrative's deeper meaning exceeds his frame.
3.26: avdohi di-el-ilai (ʿabdôhî dî-ʾēl-ʿelyāʾ): servants of the Most High God. Nebuchadnezzar calls them out using the divine title el-ilai (God Most High) — the superlative form of ilai (high/exalted). The title appears frequently in Daniel for YHWH (2:18, 19, 20, 37, 44; 3:26, 32; 4:2, 17, 24, 25, 32, 34; 5:18, 21; 7:18, 22, 25, 27; 11:36). The Gentile king's use of el-ilai is itself a form of acknowledgment: whatever the fourth figure was, YHWH is the Most High whose servants he was protecting.
3.27: The verification of the miracle by the assembled officials: la shelet nura begeshmeehon (lāʾ šĕleṭ nûrāʾ begiš-mêhôn): fire had no power over their bodies. Shelet (had power/authority) is a key word in Daniel — the same root used for those who have dominion, authority, power. Fire's authority is denied: it could not exercise its jurisdiction over YHWH's servants. The four specific statements (“not singed, garments unchanged, no smell of fire”) are legally precise testimony — a formal verification of the miracle.
3.28: malʾakheh (malʾakhêhî): his angel. Nebuchadnezzar now identifies the fourth figure as YHWH's angel rather than a generic divine being — a shift from bar-elaheen (son of the gods) to malʾakheh (his angel/messenger). The verb sheziv (delivered) is the same root used by the three men in their declaration (v. 17: yakhil leshezavah — able to deliver). YHWH did exactly what they said he could do. Shenet pitgam malka (changed the king's word) — the king's decree was not annulled but made irrelevant: the fire obeyed YHWH, not the king.
3.29: Nebuchadnezzar's decree protecting the servants of YHWH: umeshta (ûmešnāʾ: to be dismembered) and baitehhon nevali yishtavon (their houses to be made a dunghill). The punishment for blasphemy against YHWH is given in terms of the standard Babylonian execution-and-property-confiscation formula. The Aramaic negida (decree/ruling) is a royal edict with universal force. Notably, Nebuchadnezzar does not convert: he issues a decree of toleration, not a confession of monotheism.
3.30: tzalach (ṣĕlaḥ): promoted/prospered. The verb root tsalach means to advance, prosper, or progress — used in Ezra 5-6 for the progress of the temple-building. The three men are promoted (harbit = advanced to greater status) in the province of Babylon. The narrative ends with worldly vindication — the same pattern as Daniel himself in ch. 2: faithfulness leads to promotion. The literary structure is chiastic: forced to appear before the king (v. 13) → thrown into furnace → vindicated and promoted before the king (v. 30).

4.1: nebukhad-netstsar malka lekhol-amaya umevaya ulishanaya (nĕḇûḵad-neṣṣar malkāʾ lĕḵol-ʿammayyāʾ ûmĕʾayyāʾ wĕliššānayyāʾ): Nebuchadnezzar the king to all peoples, nations, and languages. Chapter 4 is unique in Scripture: a Gentile king's first-person testimony and praise of YHWH, framed as a royal letter to the whole empire. The Aramaic word shelamkhon yisgeh (may your peace increase) echoes the standard greeting of royal Aramaic letters (identical to the Elephantine papyri and Ezra's correspondence). The literary form authenticates the setting.
4.2: atayya v'timhaya (ʾāṯayyāʾ wĕṯimhāyyāʾ): signs and wonders. The Aramaic pair is the standard formula for divine acts of power — used consistently in Daniel (6:27-28) and paralleled in Acts 2:22 for Christ's mighty works.
