Shaphir qadamai (it is a good thing before me): the king judges that his testimony is worthy — a self-aware editorial note from the narrator (Nebuchadnezzar speaking as if from a position of restored sanity and faith).
4.3: malkhuteh malkhut alam (malkûṯêh malkûṯ ʿālam): his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom. The Aramaic alam (world/eternity) is cognate to the Hebrew olam. This confession in v. 3 is repeated almost verbatim in v. 34 after Nebuchadnezzar's humiliation — it frames the chapter: the king knows the truth before his madness and confesses it again after. The phrase recurs at 7:14 for the Son of Man's dominion — the eternal kingdom language reaches its Christological fulfillment there.
4.4: shale (šĕlēh): at ease, prospering, tranquil. The Aramaic root shelah (peace/security) describes Nebuchadnezzar's state before the vision. Raʿnan (flourishing/luxuriant) is a word normally applied to trees and plants — used here metaphorically of the king's prosperity, foreshadowing the tree-image of vv. 10-12. The dream comes in this moment of peak prosperity: the highest point of human achievement becomes the site of divine intervention.
4.5: khezve de-yalbunani (ḥĕzwê dî-yḇalʿunannî): dreams that terrified me. The Aramaic root bela (swallow/terrify) is vivid — the dreams consumed him. Dimyonaya al-mishkevi (images upon my bed) uses the plural dimyona (imaginings/visions) for the frightening content. The juxtaposition of prosperity (v. 4) and terror (v. 5) is the narrative hinge: success does not protect from divine warning.
4.6: belteshatzar rav kharshaya (bēlṭĕšaṣṣar rab ḥarṭummayāʾ): Belteshazzar, chief of the magicians. Daniel's Babylonian court name (belteshatzar = “Bel protect his life” or “may Bel protect the king”) is used by the narrator here in the king's speech — but the Aramaic adds di-ruach elahim qadishim beh (in whom is the spirit of the holy gods), the same formula the queen uses in 5:11. The court name conceals a Hebrew reality; the spirit-formula reveals it.
4.7: v'acharon ul Daniel (wĕʾaḥărîn ʿāl Dāniyyēʾl): and finally Daniel came in. The detail that Daniel comes in last — after all the other wise men have failed — heightens his presentation as the one who alone has the spirit. The narrative echoes ch. 2: the wise men fail, Daniel succeeds. Pesha (interpretation) is the Aramaic technical term for dream-interpretation used throughout ch. 2 and 4-5.
4.8: di-ruach elahim qadishim beh (dî-rûaḥ ʾĕlāhîn qaddîšîn bēhî): in whom is the spirit of the holy gods. The plural elahim qadishim (holy gods) again reflects the king's polytheistic frame, but his consistent use of this formula for Daniel suggests he has identified Daniel with a unique divine source. The same phrase appears in 5:11, 14 — it is the queen's formula and then the king's. The spirit that distinguishes Daniel from all others is YHWH's spirit, misidentified by the Babylonian frame as the spirit of “holy gods” (plural).
4.9: rav kharshaya (rab ḥarṭummayāʾ): master of the magicians. The Aramaic chartom (magician/diviner) is possibly an Egyptian loanword (ḥr-tp or similar) carried into the ANE court tradition. Nebuchadnezzar addresses Daniel as the superior of the court wisdom class — but then appeals to the spirit that transcends that class. The address la ya'aqer manach raz (no secret troubles you) establishes Daniel's competence before the request.
4.10: ilana begoah shemayya (ʾîlānāʾ begoah šemayyāʾ): the tree in the middle of the earth / at the height of heaven. The dream-vision opens with the cosmic tree motif familiar from Mesopotamian iconography: a world-tree whose branches reach heaven, whose roots go deep. The Aramaic ilana (tree) appears seven times in vv. 10-23. The cosmic tree is a standard image for empire in ANE literature — cf. Ezek 31:3-9 (Assyria as cedars of Lebanon). The height reaching heaven (rugbeh limshet lishemayya) echoes the tower of Babel (ve-rosho vashamayim, Gen 11:4).
4.11: sgav ilana v'taqef (sgā ʾîlānāʾ wetqēp̄): the tree grew tall and became strong. The two verbs — sga (to grow large/magnify) and tqep (to become strong) — describe a progressive intensification. Ad-shamayya (to the heavens): Aramaic shamayya (the heavens) is the same word in Aramaic and Hebrew. The visibility clause — “it was visible to the end of all the earth” — is imperial hyperbole appropriate for a world-empire: what reaches to heaven is visible everywhere.
4.12: The tree providing food and shelter for all creatures: tepeh saggi (abundant fruit), meta lesavah lekhola (food for all). The tree as provider of food, shade, and shelter echoes the parable of the mustard seed (Matt 13:32) — a tree under which the birds of the air nest. The Babylonian tree-image (power as shelter) becomes in the NT the Christ-image (kingdom as shelter). The Aramaic detail that “all flesh was fed from it” (kol-beshar yitnezuun minneh) is universal — the tree feeds every living thing.
4.13: ir v'qaddish (ʿîr wĕqaddîš): a watcher and a holy one. The ir (watcher/waking one) is an angelic being unique to Daniel in the canonical OT (though appearing in the Enoch literature). The term iqad (watcher/one who watches) suggests a heavenly being who observes and enforces divine decisions. Paired with qaddish (holy one), it is a doublet for a single angelic figure. The heavenly court language that dominates chs. 4-5 and 7 positions YHWH as the true king whose heavenly council issues binding decrees over human kingdoms.
4.14: gadu ilana (gûdû ʾîlānāʾ): cut down the tree. The imperative gadu is from the root geda (to cut down) — the same verb used in the OT for felling trees (Deut 19:5). The watcher's decree is juridical: badar periage (scatter its fruit) — the reversal of the tree's provision. The stump is left with a band of iron and bronze: ikar shorshohi b'ara (leave its roots/stump in the earth) — a crucial detail that signals the judgment is not final elimination but temporary humiliation with restoration in view.
4.15: ikar shorshohi (ʾiqqar šorĕšôhî): the stump of its roots. Ikar is the tree-stump — what remains after felling. The binding of the stump with iron and bronze (be-essar di-parzel unechash) prevents regrowth during the seven times. Then the pivotal pronoun shift: “let his heart be changed from a man's, and let an animal's heart be given to him” — the tree suddenly becomes the king. The Aramaic levav anasha (heart of a man) → levav chaiva (heart of an animal) describes the loss of rational dignity (tselem elahim, the image of God in human terms).
4.16: levav enasha yishnon mineh (libbēhî min-ʾĕnāšāʾ yĕšannaʾ): let his heart be changed from a man's. The verb shanna (to change/alter) was used in 3:28 for the angel who changed the king's fire-decree. Now it describes the reversal of rational faculty. levav chaiva yityhav leh (an animal's heart be given to him): the passive form (yityhav = let it be given) uses the divine passive — God is the implied agent. The number shiv'a idanin (seven times/seasons) uses the ambiguous idan — period, season, time — which may mean seven years or seven seasons.
4.17: bigzerat iryin petagma uvammar qadishin shuʾleta (bigĕzêrat ʿîrîn pittĕgāmāʾ ûbimēʾmar qaddîšîn šūʾaltāʾ): by the decree of the watchers the sentence and by the word of the holy ones the decision. The juridical language of the heavenly court is precise: gezera (decree/sentence), petagam (word/decision), shuʾalta (request/demand). The heavenly council issues binding decisions about earthly kingdoms. The stated purpose: di-ydaʿ chayya di-shalit ʿilaya b'malkhut anasha (that the living may know that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men) — the theological thesis of Daniel's entire vision of history.
4.18: The king asks Daniel to interpret because kol-khakimei malkhuti la yakhlin hodaʿutani peshara (all the wise men of my kingdom could not make known to me the interpretation) — the court wisdom system has failed again (cf. ch. 2). The Aramaic hkhim (wise) is the comprehensive term for the Babylonian learned class. Daniel's superiority is framed in terms of the spirit: ruach elahim qadishim (spirit of holy gods), not superior training or skill.
4.19: Belteshatzar etdahar sha'ah akhat (bēlṭĕšaṣṣar etdahar šāʿah ḥăḏāh): Belteshazzar was alarmed for a moment. Etdahar (was alarmed/horrified) uses the same root as 4:5 for the king's terror. Sha'ah akhat (one hour/moment) — a brief pause of horror at the vision's meaning. Then the king explicitly invites Daniel's honest report: khezva ve-peshara la yevahalanek (let not the dream or its interpretation trouble you) — the king, for once, wants the truth even if it is about himself.
4.20: Daniel's description of the tree restates vv. 10-12 almost verbatim — the repetition is formal, legally confirming the interpretation against the original vision. The Aramaic narrative technique of repetition-for-confirmation appears throughout Daniel: visions are restated (2:31-35 restated in 2:38-44), decrees are restated (3:4-6 restated in 3:10-11). The mirroring of original statement and interpretation signals truthfulness and completeness.
4.21: The tree whose branches housed the birds, whose shade sheltered the animals, from whose fruit all flesh was fed: these details are restated in the interpretation to prepare for the application. Kol-beshar mineh yitnezuun (all flesh was fed from it): the universality of Nebuchadnezzar's empire-as-provision is the ground of the judgment — he used what YHWH gave him as if he had made it himself (cf. Ezek 29:3: “the Nile is mine and I made it”).
4.22: ant-hu malka (ʾantāh-hûʾ malkāʾ): it is you, O king. The application is direct and personal. The Aramaic pronoun ant-hu (you-he) is emphatic. The phrase di-revat v'etqefat (who grew great and became strong) mirrors the tree's description exactly — the king is the tree. Ve-riavat rugbak ad-shamayya (and your greatness has reached heaven): the same tower-of-Babel language that described the tree now describes Nebuchadnezzar — the echo is unmistakable.
4.23: The watcher's decree restated in summary: cut down the tree, but leave the stump. The connecting purpose (di-shalit ʿIllaya b'malkhut anasha: that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men) appears again — this is the sentence's stated rationale. The threefold repetition of this sentence (vv. 17, 25, 32) in ch. 4 is Daniel's theological drumbeat: all human sovereignty is derivative, conditional, and reversible.
4.24: dena peshara malka (dĕnāh pišrāʾ malkāʾ): this is the interpretation, O king. The formal announcement of the interpretation. Pigra Ilai et-ot malka rabayta (the decree of the Most High has reached my lord the king): the Aramaic mata (reached) suggests the decree has arrived like a legal document. The king stands under a sentence that has already been issued from the heavenly court — the only question is when it will be executed.
4.25: v'lak letardoon min anasha (wĕlāk yṭardûn min-ʾĕnāšāʾ): you will be driven from men. The passive yitardoon (will be driven) uses the divine passive — the same God who issued the decree in the heavenly court will execute it. Im-chayat bara tekhul (you will eat grass like an ox): the animal humiliation described. shiv'a idanin (seven times) restated: the duration is fixed. The purpose: di-teda di-shalit ʿIllaya (that you may know that the Most High rules) — the personal, second-person application of the theological thesis.
4.26: v'di amaru lishbaq ikar shorshohi di ilana (wĕdî ʾămarû lišbaq ʿiqqar šorĕšôhî dî-ʾîlānāʾ): and as for the command to leave the stump of the tree's roots. The stump-preservation is interpreted as the guarantee of Nebuchadnezzar's restoration: malkhutakh lekhak tiqum (your kingdom will be confirmed for you) after you know that Heaven rules. The Aramaic shamayya shalit (Heaven rules) substitutes Heaven as a circumlocution for YHWH — a reverent substitution also found in the phrase malkut shamayya (kingdom of Heaven) in the NT (Matt 3:2; 4:17).
4.27: la-ken malka mashlakhani yishtaveh (lāhēn malkāʾ mšaḥlĕḥānnî yiqqabal): therefore, O king, let my counsel be acceptable to you. Daniel's exhortation to repentance: peruk khatayak bitzidqah (break off your sins by righteousness) and avatek banot anaya (your iniquities by showing mercy to the poor). The two imperatives (pruk = break off/atone; anay = afflicted/poor) constitute a summary of OT ethics: righteousness and mercy. The conditional hope: hn tihye arokhat shlevtak (perhaps there will be a lengthening of your tranquility) — the judgment may be postponed if repentance occurs.
4.28: All this came upon Nebuchadnezzar: the narrative resumes after the interpretation with the bare statement of fulfillment. The Aramaic kol-dena mata (all this came) uses the verb meta (to arrive/come) — the decree from the heavenly court has arrived. The time marker leqets yarkheen tri-eshar (at the end of twelve months) shows the delay: one full year elapsed between the dream/interpretation and the judgment. Daniel's exhortation (v. 27) had been followed — at least temporarily.
4.29: al heikhal malkhuta di-Bavel mehhalekh havah (ʿal hêḵal malkûṯāʾ dî-bābel mĕhallēk hăwāh): on the palace of the kingdom of Babylon he was walking. The setting of the boast: the roof of the palace, looking over the city. Mihallekh havah (was walking) uses the Aramaic participial construction for ongoing action — he was strolling, luxuriating in the view. The timing — at the end of the twelve months, during a moment of prosperous ease — is the narrative's ironic precision: the judgment falls at the height of complacency.
4.30: la-da Bavel rabta di-ana benayit (hălāʾ dāʾ bābel rabbĕṯāʾ dî-ʾănāh bĕnayit): Is not this great Babylon that I built? The boast contains three claims: ana benayit (I built), livet malkhuta (for the house of the kingdom), bitqef khutsani (by my mighty power). Historical confirmation: Nebuchadnezzar was the greatest builder-king in Babylonian history; the surviving texts of his building inscriptions use nearly identical language (“I built Babylon, the great city, my royal residence”). The irony is that his own inscriptions confirm the accuracy of the biblical portrait — and their boast is the sin Daniel warned against.
4.31: od pithgama b'pum malka (ʿôḏ millĕṯāʾ bepum malkāʾ): while the word was still in the king's mouth. The immediacy is the literary point: the divine verdict falls before the sentence is finished. The Aramaic bat qal nefal min-shamayya (a voice fell from heaven) — bat qal (daughter of the voice) is the technical term in rabbinic literature for a divine voice from heaven, lesser than prophecy but still authoritative. The voice declares: malkhuta avadat minnakh (the kingdom has departed from you) — a perfect/completed declaration.
4.32: The prophetic declaration is immediate and specific: min-anasha tetarad (you will be driven from men), im-chayat bara mevakhak lakh (with the animals of the field your dwelling will be). The threefold repetition of shiv'a idanin (seven times) throughout the chapter has built to this moment of execution. The declarative purpose — di-teda di-shalit ʿIllaya b'malkhut anasha (that you may know that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men) — echoes vv. 17 and 25. The purpose of YHWH's judgment is always revelation.
4.33: ba-shetaʿa fulfilled upon Nebuchadnezzar (beh-šaʿtāʾ millĕṯāʾ sāpat ʿal nĕḇûḵaḏ-neṣṣar): at that very hour the word was fulfilled. The Aramaic ba-shetaʿa (at that hour/moment) is emphatic immediacy — no delay between sentence and execution. The medical symptoms described (min-anasha tetarad, v'asba chaiva yikhul, u-ketal-shamayya yitsatabah: driven from men, eating grass like cattle, his body bathed in the dew of heaven) may reflect the condition known as boanthropy — a psychiatric syndrome documented in ancient Near Eastern medical texts and confirmed by modern case studies.
4.34: ulqats yomaya ana nebukhad-netstsar einai lishemayya natlet (ûliqṣāṯ yômayāʾ ʾănāh nĕḇûḵaḏ-neṣṣar ʿênay lišmayyāʾ nĕṭĕlēṯ): and at the end of the days I, Nebuchadnezzar, lifted my eyes to heaven. The raising of eyes to heaven is the recovery of the rational-and-spiritual faculty: umdaʿi yetuv ʿalai (and my reason returned to me). The first act of restored reason is the praise of YHWH: ulilaiay berakhit (and I blessed the Most High). The verb berakh (bless/kneel) signals worship-posture. The confession that follows: malkhuteh malkhut alam v'shaltehneh im-dar v'dar (his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom and his dominion is from generation to generation) — the same formula as v. 3, now confessed from experience rather than introduced as preface.
4.35: v'kol-dayrei arʿa k'la khashiveen (wĕḵol-dāyĕrê ʾarʿāʾ k'lāʾ ḥăšîḇîn): all the inhabitants of the earth are accounted as nothing. K'la (as nothing/zero) is absolute: compared to YHWH's sovereignty, all earthly inhabitors are nothing. The phrase v'kitsvehteh ʿaved bishemayya uvimdinat arʿa (and he does his will in the host of heaven and among the inhabitants of earth) uses the verb ʿaved (does/makes) — YHWH's will is executed in both the heavenly and earthly realms simultaneously. The rhetorical question v'la-itai man di-yemacher b'yedehheh (there is no one who can stay his hand) closes with absolute unilateral sovereignty.
4.36: b'idana dena madaʿi yetub ʿalai (bizdān dĕnāh maddi ʿalai ytûb): at the same time my reason returned to me. The restoration formula is threefold: hadaru heder malkhuti (the majesty of my kingdom was restored), v'labibeday itub lehon (and my reason returned to me), and yattirey revavet (I was greatly increased). The Aramaic hadar (splendor/majesty) is a royal term — the same word used for YHWH's glory in the Psalms (Ps 29:4; 96:6). The restoration of human dignity is described in the same terms as divine glory — another echo of the tselem elahim (image of God) principle.
4.37: kaan ana nebukhad-netstsar meshabbech u-meromem umehaddra le-malik shemayya (kĕʿan ʾănāh nĕḇûḵaḏ-neṣṣar mĕšabbaḥ ûmĕrômem ûmĕhaddêr lĕmelek šemayyāʾ): now I, Nebuchadnezzar, praise and extol and honor the King of Heaven. The triple participle construction (meshabbech, meromem, mehaddra = praising, extolling, honoring) is a liturgical formula — the language of worship applied to YHWH by a Gentile king. The final confession: di-ʿavdeh qeshet (whose works are truth) and urkhev-oteh din (whose ways are justice) — both attributes of YHWH throughout the OT. The closing warning: v'di-mehalkheen begava yakhil le-heshpalah (those who walk in pride he is able to humble) — using the verb yakhil (is able) that the three men used of YHWH's ability to deliver them.

5.1: Belshatzar malka ʿabad lechem rab (bēlšaṣṣar malkāʾ ʿăḇaḏ leḥem raḇ): Belshazzar the king made a great feast. Historical note: Belshazzar is now confirmed as a genuine Babylonian figure through the Nabonidus Chronicle and the Verse Account of Nabonidus — he served as co-regent with his father Nabonidus. His title is son of Nebuchadnezzar (v. 2, 11) in the Semitic sense of dynasty-descendant or successor, not literal son. The feast of a thousand: alef rabrevaneh (a thousand of his nobles) — the hyperbolic number of royal grandeur. The historical night is likely October 539 BCE when Cyrus's troops diverted the Euphrates and entered Babylon.
5.2: taam (ṭĕʿēm): taste/order/edict. The Aramaic taam (taste/judgment/decree) suggests a royal command issued under the influence of wine. The vessels of the Jerusalem temple (mano di-hekhla di-beyt-elaha di-birushalem) are specifically identified: mano (vessels/utensils), hekhla (temple). The desecration of the sacred vessels connects to Nebuchadnezzar's original seizure (Dan 1:2; cf. Ezra 1:7-11) — the vessels taken as war-trophies are now pressed into profane banquet use, escalating the sacrilege.
5.3: The active desecration: v'eshtu vahon malka virabrevaneh (and they drank from them, the king and his nobles). The plural includes the concubines and wives (shegalateh unilkhateh). The drinking scene is a deliberate contrast with the earlier narrative: Nebuchadnezzar at least worshipped a god (the gold image); Belshazzar reduces worship to a feast-prop. The vessels become vessels of judgment: what was used to defile becomes the occasion for divine response.
5.4: shabbahu le-elahei dahava vekhaspah (šabbaḥû lēʾlāhê dahabāʾ wĕḵasp̄āʾ): they praised the gods of gold and silver. The list of six materials (gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, stone) mirrors the statue of Dan 2 — deliberately invoking Nebuchadnezzar's dream and its interpretation. The six materials correspond to the six kingdoms of the ch. 2 vision. The feast that praises these material gods while desecrating YHWH's temple-vessels is the concentrated expression of what Daniel 2 condemned: human kingdoms that mistake their own material glory for ultimate reality.
5.5: etsba din alaha (ʾeṣḇāʾ dî yad ʾĕnāšāʾ): fingers of a man's hand. The vision emerges from the wall: ketefat gira di-kedasha di-hechal malka (opposite the lampstand, on the plaster of the wall of the king's palace). The lampstand (nehora, light) illuminates the scene — the writing appears where it can be seen by all. The Aramaic pars khetav (wrote writing) emphasizes both the action and the product: the divine hand writes and the writing stands.
5.6: be-edin malka zivohi shennoi (bēʾḏayin malkāʾ ziwnôhî šennaw): then the king's countenance changed. The verb shennoi (was changed) is the same root used in 3:19 for Nebuchadnezzar's changed countenance and in 4:16 for the changed heart — Daniel's Aramaic consistently uses this root for radical transformation under divine pressure. Reʾyaneh yevahaloneh (his thoughts alarmed him): the verb behal (to terrify/disturb) appears throughout the chapter (5:9, 10) — the same root as ch. 4's king-in-terror. The physical symptoms — loosened loins, knocking knees — are the diplomatic language for total collapse of composure.
5.7: Belshazzar cries out for the same ashpaya vegadzraya khaldaya (astrologers, Chaldeans, diviners) who have failed in every previous crisis (chs. 2, 4). The reward offered is: arganev-an talbash v'hemnikhta di-dahava al-tzawareh (clothed in purple and a chain of gold around his neck) and telita b'malkhuta ishalat (third ruler in the kingdom) — consistent with Belshazzar's position as co-regent: he can only offer third place, not second. This is historically precise and archaeologically confirmed.
5.8: The failure of the wise men: la yakhallin khetava lemeqra (could not read the writing) or give interpretation. The Aramaic structure parallels ch. 2 and ch. 4 — the court wisdom system fails identically each time. The literary pattern says: there is a category of knowledge that human wisdom systems cannot access. Peshara lehodat (to make known the interpretation) — the verb ydaʿ (to know/make known) is the epistemological key: Babylonian wisdom cannot produce the knowledge YHWH alone gives.
5.9: be-edin malka Belshatzar saggi metvahel (ʾeḏayin malkāʾ bēlšaṣṣar saggî mĕṯbahal): then King Belshazzar was greatly troubled. The superlative saggi (greatly) intensifies the already-intense verb. Zivohi shannyin ʿalohay (his countenance was changing upon him) — again the root shannyi. V'rabrevaneh metvahalin (and his nobles were troubled) — the fear spreads from the king to the court. The banquet that began with triumph ends in terror: the vessels of YHWH have become the vessels of judgment.
5.10: malketa liqbel milei malka v-rabrevaneh leveyt mishtaya ʿalit (malkĕṯāʾ liqbēl millê malkāʾ wĕrabrĕwānôhî lĕḇêṯ mišĕtāyāʾ ʿāllat): the queen, because of the words of the king and his nobles, came into the banqueting hall. The queen who enters is likely the queen-mother — Nitocris or a senior queen with knowledge of Nebuchadnezzar's court (v. 11: “in the days of your father”). Her composure — malketa lealam kheyey (O king, live forever) — contrasts with the general alarm. She has information the current court lacks.
5.11: itai gever b'malkhutakh di-ruach elahim qadishim beh (ʾîṯay gĕḇar bĕmalkûṯāk dî-rûaḥ ʾĕlāhîn qaddîšîn bēhî): there is a man in your kingdom in whom is the spirit of the holy gods. The queen-mother's testimony establishes Daniel's credentials by reference to Nebuchadnezzar's own evaluation. Three terms are used for Nebuchadnezzar's recognition of Daniel: nahor (light/illumination), sekhlet (insight), and khokhmat elahim (wisdom of the gods) — the same terms appear in the original court examination (1:20). The spirit that was present in Nebuchadnezzar's day is still present in Daniel: divine gifts are permanent, not generational.
5.12: ruach yatira v'mandaʿ v'sekhlet lefeysher khelmin (rûaḥ yattîrāh ûmandaʿ ûśokletānû lĕp̄ašar ḥelmîn): an extraordinary spirit and knowledge and understanding to interpret dreams. The list of Daniel's abilities: interpreting dreams, solving riddles (mefesha udah), and loosening knots (meshare qatrein). The Aramaic qatrein (knots/difficulties) is a vivid metaphor for complex problems — the same image appears in modern Arabic. Daniel's name is given by the queen: Belteshatzar (his court name) — she uses it to identify him to a king who may not have known him personally.
5.13: Daniel is brought before the king. The king's address begins with a biographical note: ant-hu Daniel di-min-benei galuta di-yehud (are you Daniel who is from the exiles of Judah?). The identification of Daniel as an exile (galuta) is politically precise: he has no natural power-base, no dynastic claim, no court loyalty by birth. His status as exile makes his authority all the more clearly supernatural. The king knows Daniel's origin — this is not ignorance but the beginning of an interrogation designed to assess reliability.
5.14: The king's statement of what he has heard about Daniel: v'shemet ʿalakh di ruach elahim bakh (and I have heard of you that the spirit of God is in you). The king uses elahim (God/gods) without qualification here — a more personal phrasing than the queen-mother's elahim qadishim (holy gods). The three-part description of Daniel's gifts: nahor v'sekhlet v'khokhmat yatira (light and understanding and excellent wisdom) is identical to the queen's formulation in v. 11. Belshazzar has received the testimony accurately.
5.15: The narrative irony is made explicit: the wise men and astrologers who were brought in could not read the writing or make known the interpretation — v'peshara la-yekholeen le-hodat. The Aramaic la-yekholeen (could not) uses the same root (yakhil) that the three men used for YHWH's ability (3:17) and that Nebuchadnezzar used in his final doxology (4:37). The wise men lack the ability that YHWH possesses and grants to his servants.
5.16: v'enant shemaʿet ʿalakh di-tukhhal pishrin lemifsar (wʾănāh šĕmaʿet ʿălāk dî tûḵal pišrîn lĕmip̄sar): and I have heard of you that you can give interpretations. The conditional invitation: kean hn tukhhal khetava lemeqra (now if you can read the writing). The offer is repeated: purple, gold chain, third-ruler status. The political offer remains operative even after the wise men have failed — Belshazzar is desperate enough to offer the same package to anyone who can succeed.
5.17: be-edin Daniel enah umreh qodam malka (bēʾḏayin dāniyyēʾl ʿānēh wĕʾāmar qŏḏām malkāʾ): then Daniel answered and said before the king. Daniel's response opens with a formal refusal of the gifts: matnetak lakh yehavu v'yahavatakh le-akhra hab (your gifts be for yourself, give your rewards to another). This is a striking posture: Daniel refuses payment before delivering the interpretation, ensuring his independence from the court system. The interpretation will not be shaped by the reward — a principled stance that contrasts with the court wise men whose interpretations followed the money.
5.18: anta malka elaha ilaya malkhuta v-revuta v-iqqara v-hadar yahav le-nebukhad-netstsar avukh (ʾantāh malkāʾ ʾĕlāhāʾ ʿelyāʾ malkûṯāʾ wĕrəḇûṯāʾ wʾiqqārāʾ wĕhaḏārāʾ yĕhab lĕnĕḇûḵaḏ-neṣṣar ʾăḇûhāk): you, O king — God Most High gave Nebuchadnezzar your father kingship and greatness and glory and majesty. The fourfold listing (malkuta, revuta, iqqara, hadar = kingship, greatness, honor, glory) uses all the royal-dignity vocabulary of Daniel. The attribution to God Most High (elaha ilaya) establishes immediately that Nebuchadnezzar's power was gift, not achievement — the ground for Belshazzar's guilt in v. 22.
5.19: min-qdam revuteh di-yahav leh kol-amayya umevaya ulishanaya hayu zaʿin v-daylin (min-qŏḏam gəḇûrəṯēh … kol-ʿammayyāʾ ûmĕʾayyāʾ wĕliššānayyāʾ): before his greatness that he gave him, all peoples, nations, and languages trembled and feared before him. The triadic formula of peoples-nations-languages appears again (cf. 3:4; 4:1) — the scope of Nebuchadnezzar's terror was universal. V-di hava tsave haval (and whom he willed he killed): the absolute power is described without euphemism. This absolute power was YHWH's gift — which makes its abuse in Belshazzar all the more culpable (v. 22).
5.20: v-kivat levaveh v-ruaheh itkeshiat lihzada (wûkḇar libbēhî wĕrûḥēhî tiqĕp̄at lĕhazāḏāʾ): and when his heart was lifted up and his spirit became proud. The Aramaic kevat (hardened/stiffened) of the heart is a strong image — the heart becomes rigid, inflexible. The verb tikhep (became strong/proud) uses the same root as tikhep in the tree-image (“became strong,” 4:11) — Nebuchadnezzar's strength became Nebuchadnezzar's pride, and now it is the cause of his fall. The parallel to Belshazzar makes the typological point: the pattern of pride-judgment repeats.
5.21: Daniel's summary of Nebuchadnezzar's humiliation, echoing ch. 4: min-benei anasha tetarad (driven from among men), levaveh im-chayata shevi (his heart was made like the animals), im-radana manveh (his dwelling was with wild asses). The purpose: di-yidaʿ di-shalit elaha ilaya b'malkhut anasha (that he might know that God Most High rules in the kingdom of men). The theological thesis is restated for the fifth time in chs. 4-5: Nebuchadnezzar learned it the hard way; Belshazzar had the testimony of that lesson and ignored it.
5.22: v-ant bereh Belshatzar la-heshpalat levavakh (wʾantāh bĕrēhî bēlšaṣṣar lāʾ heš-pālət libbāk): and you his son Belshazzar have not humbled your heart. This is the indictment. The verb heshpalat (humbled) uses the Haphel stem of the root shapel (to be low/humble) — the same root used throughout Daniel for YHWH's ability to humble the proud (4:37). The conjunction kol-qobel di-kol-dena yadaʿta (though you knew all this) makes the guilt inexcusable: unlike the nations who had no testimony, Belshazzar had the full record of Nebuchadnezzar's experience and disregarded it.
5.23: v-ʿal mar-shamayya hitromamta (wʾal māraʾ-šemayyāʾ hitrommāmta): and against the Lord of Heaven you have exalted yourself. The verb hitromamta (exalted yourself) uses the Hitpael of ram (to be high) — reflexive self-exaltation against the Lord of Heaven (mar-shamayya, a title for YHWH found only here in Daniel and Ezra 1:2). The three desecrations named: manohi di-veiteh (vessels of his house), veant verabrevanakh shegalatakh v-nilkhatakh shatin bahon (and you and your nobles and wives drinking from them), velilahei kaspa vedahava … shabbacht (and you have praised gods of silver and gold). The triple indictment escalates: desecrating what is holy, using the holy for debauchery, replacing YHWH with material gods.
5.24: min-kodamoh shallikh pasa yada (min-qŏḏāmôhî šĕliḥ pāsāʾ yādāʾ): from his presence was sent the hand, the palm. The Aramaic pasa (palm of the hand) is specific — not a full hand, just the palm with fingers. Shallikh (was sent) uses the divine passive: God sent it. The writing that was inscribed: kehtava dena resham (this writing was inscribed). The divine authorship of the writing is stated without qualification — the question is only whether anyone can read it.
5.25: The inscription: mene mene teqel ufarsin (mĕnēʾ mĕnēʾ tĕqēl ûp̄arsîn). These are Aramaic words with multiple semantic registers. Mene is from the root mana (to count/number/assign). Teqel is from teqal (to weigh). Parsin is the plural of peres (half-mina, or to divide) — also a pun on Paras (Persia). The doubling of mene may intensify the meaning (numbered, numbered — it is truly numbered), paralleling the doubled dreams of Genesis (41:32: “the dream was doubled because the thing is fixed by God”). The inscription can also be read as a set of monetary units (mina, mina, shekel, and half-minas) — devaluing kingdoms in a monetary metaphor.
5.26: dena peshara milta mene (dĕnāh p̄išrāh millĕṯāʾ mĕnēʾ): this is the interpretation of the word — mene. Manah elaha malkhutakh v-hashlamah (God has numbered your kingdom and brought it to an end). The verb manah (to number/count) is completed action: it is done, the count is finished. The Hiphil hashlamah (brought to completion/an end) uses shalam — the same root as shalom (peace/wholeness/completeness). The irony: YHWH brings Belshazzar's kingdom to shalom — its complete end, its final state — using the peace-vocabulary.
5.27: teqel tqilta b-moznaya v-hishtkakhat khasir (tĕqēl tĕqîltāh bĕmôznayyāʾ wĕhišṭĕkaḥat ḥassîr): teqel — you have been weighed in the balances and found wanting. The passive tqilta (you were weighed) and hishtkakhat (you were found) both use divine passives. The balance/scale (moznayyah) is the judicial metaphor for divine assessment — cf. Ps 62:9 (men are a breath, in the balances they go up). Khasir (deficient/lacking/wanting): the verdict is not that he is evil but that he is insufficient — his kingdom lacks the weight, the substance, to endure. The moral and political assessment coincide in Aramaic khasir.
5.28: peres peliget malkhutakh (pĕrēs pĕligat malkûṯāk): peres — your kingdom is divided. The singular peres (divide) now applies to both the monetary unit (half) and the geopolitical act. V-yehivat liMadai u-Pharas (and given to the Medes and Persians): the pun is complete — peres (divide) = Paras (Persia). The kingdom that praised the gods of gold, silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone (v. 4) is itself divided like a coin. Daniel's interpretation converts three monetary nouns into three judicial sentences: counted, weighed, divided — a complete legal verdict in three words.
5.29: Belshazzar's response: he honors Daniel with the purple, chain, and proclamation of third-ruler status — the promised reward (v. 7) is paid even though the interpretation is a death sentence. This is a peculiar detail that rings historically true: the king honors the interpreter despite the interpretation. The Aramaic v-karaz ʿalohi (and they proclaimed concerning him) uses the same verb karaz (herald/proclaim) as the herald in 3:4 — the same word that announced the command to worship the image now announces the elevation of the man who refused to be ruled by the system that image represented.
5.30: beh be-leilya qetil Belshatzar (bêhî balēlyāʾ qĕṭîl bēlšaṣṣar): that very night Belshazzar the Chaldean king was killed. The Aramaic beh be-leilya (that very night) uses the doubled temporal expression for absolute immediacy — the same night, not the next day. Qetil (was killed) is the Peal passive — the simple statement of fact, without dramatic elaboration. The word kasday malka (Chaldean king) is the narrator's final epitaph — he is remembered as a Chaldean king, not as a god-fearer. The historical record: Xenophon's Cyropaedia and Herodotus both mention the capture of Babylon during a night feast.
5.31: v-Daryavesh Madaya qabbel malkhuta (wĕdāryāwēš māḏāʾāh qabbēl malkûṯāʾ): and Darius the Mede received the kingdom. The verb qabbel (received/accepted) — the kingdom passes, not by conquest described dramatically, but by quiet transfer. Bar-shinin shittin u-tarteen (being about sixty-two years old): the specific age is either a historical detail or a number significant for the narrative (sixty-two years after Nebuchadnezzar's accession would correspond roughly to 561 BCE; the narrative would then be ca. 539 BCE, within 22 years). The chapter ends not with Cyrus but with Darius the Mede — the figure around whom the next narrative (ch. 6) will turn.

10.1: dabar emet (dābār ʾěmet): 'a true word/message.' Emet (H571, truth/faithfulness/reliability) heads the characterization of the vision — this is not ambiguous prophecy but verified, faithful disclosure. The term tsava gadol (great conflict) signals that the vision concerns cosmic warfare beyond the political surface. Daniel mourning for three weeks (v.2-3) is a prolonged preparation for major revelation — cf. Moses on Sinai forty days (Exod 24:18).
10.2: hayiti mitabel shloshah shavuim yamim: 'I was mourning for three weeks of days.' Mitabel (H56, mourn): intense sustained grief — Daniel neither eats delicacies nor meat nor wine nor anoints himself for three weeks. The mourning is over the unresolved situation of the exile and the content of the Jeremiah-calculation (ch.9). The discipline of extended mourning-prayer as preparation for revelation is a model of prophetic intercession: the body's self-denial corresponds to the spirit's urgent seeking.
10.3: lechem chamudot lo achalti uvesar vayyayin lo ba bephi: 'I ate no delicacies, no meat or wine came to my mouth, nor did I anoint myself.' The three abstentions — pleasant food, meat, wine, anointing — reverse the court-feast context of Daniel 1:5. The same court setting that Daniel navigated in ch.1 is now voluntarily renounced in mourning. Lechem chamudot (desirable/pleasant bread) recalls the chamudot term applied to Daniel himself as the beloved one — here he abstains from what is desirable in order to seek the heavenly beloved.
10.4: vaani hayiti al-yad hanahar haGadol hu Deqel: 'I was on the bank of the great river, that is, the Tigris.' Deqel (Tigris) — the Tigris rather than the Euphrates as the setting of this vision, as opposed to the Ulai canal of ch. 8. The great rivers of the ancient Near East are consistently liminal spaces for divine encounter in the prophets: Ezekiel's Chebar canal (Ezek 1:1), Ezra's Ahava canal (Ezra 8:21). Rivers mark the boundary between ordinary and sacred space.
10.5: ish echad lavush badim umotanav chagur betaz Uphaz: 'A man clothed in linen, his waist girded with gold from Uphaz.' Badim (H906, linen): the priestly material par excellence — the high priest wore linen in the holy of holies (Lev 16:4); the divine attendants in Ezekiel's vision wore linen (Ezek 9:2; 10:6-7). The figure's clothing signals priestly-divine status. Uphaz: location uncertain — possibly equivalent to Ophir (H211), the OT's gold standard. The Revelation's Son of Man figure (Rev 1:13) wears the identical garment: endedumenos podere (clothed to the foot) with a golden girdle — a direct Danielic allusion.
10.6: gevo keTarshish ufanav kemareh baraq ve'einav kelappidei esh: 'His body was like Tarshish stone, his face was like the appearance of lightning, his eyes were like torches of fire.' Tarshish (H8658) is likely beryl/chrysolite — a gleaming, translucent green-blue gem. The sequence of cosmic comparisons — lightning-face, fire-eyes, burnished-bronze limbs, thunder-voice — marks the figure as superhuman, aligned with Ezekiel's divine chariot-throne imagery (Ezek 1:7, 13-14, 27). John's vision of the risen Christ (Rev 1:14-16) replicates these features exactly, identifying the Danielic heavenly figure with the glorified Son of Man.
10.7: velo ra'u et-hamareh ha'anashim asher hayu imi: 'The men who were with me did not see the vision.' The selectivity of prophetic vision — others nearby yet unseeing — parallels Paul's Damascus road experience (Acts 9:7: men heard the voice but saw nothing; Acts 22:9: saw the light but did not hear the voice). The inability of ordinary perception to receive extraordinary divine disclosure is a consistent feature of biblical theophany: vision is given to those prepared to receive it.
10.8: ve'ani nisharthi levaddi va'ereh et-hamareh hagedolah hazot velo nithar bi koach vehodi nehavkha alai lemashchit: 'I was left alone and saw this great vision, and no strength remained in me; my radiant appearance was fearfully changed.' Hod (H1935, majesty/radiance/splendor) — the same term used for Moses's face-radiance after encountering YHWH (Exod 34:29 uses qaran, but hod describes the residual divine glory in figures). The contact with divine majesty depletes human vitality — the body cannot sustain the proximity of the holy. This is not illness but the finite's response to the infinite.
10.9: va'eshma et-qol devarav uveshomyi et-qol devarav hayiti nirdam: 'I heard the sound of his words, and when I heard the sound of his words I fell into a deep sleep on my face.' Qol devarav (sound/voice of his words): the heavenly voice as overwhelming sonic force — the same as Ezekiel's voice like many waters (Ezek 1:24). Nirdam (H7290, fell into deep sleep): not ordinary sleep but the stupor of prophetic trance, parallel to the tardemah (deep sleep) that fell on Abraham at the covenant ceremony (Gen 15:12). The posture — face to the ground — is the standard theophany response.
10.10: vehinei yad nag'ah vi vatenioani al-birkai vekaph yadai: 'And behold, a hand touched me and set me trembling on my hands and knees.' The sequence of restoration — touch, rising to knees and hands, then upright (v.11) — is a measured reinstatement of the prophet's capacity to receive the revelation. The divine touch that prostrates (overwhelming glory) and restores (enabling grace) is the pattern of all prophetic commissioning: Moses at the burning bush, Isaiah before the throne, Paul on the Damascus road.
10.11: ish chamudot bina bidevari: 'O man greatly beloved, understand the words that I speak to you.' This is the second occurrence of ish chamudot (man of desirings/beloved man) — Daniel is identified by his belovedness as the qualifier for receiving revelation. Bina (H998, understand/discern) — the imperative to discern, not merely receive, marks the prophetic task. Understanding requires active engagement, not passive reception.
10.12: al-tira Daniel ki min-hayom harishon asher natata et-libbecha lehavin velehitanot lifnei Eloheicha nishmeu devareicha: 'Do not be afraid, Daniel, for from the first day that you set your heart to understand and to humble yourself before your God, your words have been heard.' Natata et-libbecha (set your heart): lev (heart) as the whole inner life directed toward understanding. Lehitanot (to humble yourself): the reflexive Hitpael of anah — self-humbling as the disposition that opens heavenly access. The revelation was triggered on day one; the twenty-one-day delay was due to cosmic resistance (v.13), not divine inattention.
10.13: sar malkhut Paras omed lenegdi echad ve'esrim yom: 'The prince of the kingdom of Persia stood in opposition against me for twenty-one days.' Sar (H8269, prince/captain/commander) as a cosmic role — each nation has a heavenly sar (angelic guardian) corresponding to its earthly political identity. This doctrine of national angels (cf. Deut 32:8 LXX, which assigns nations to the sons of God; 1 Enoch 20) explains the geopolitical conflicts of history as having a heavenly dimension. Micha'el achad hasarim hariishonim (Michael, one of the chief princes): Michael is the guardian of Israel (12:1), distinguished as a chief-prince among the angelic order.
10.14: ubate anah lehaskil et asher-yiqreh le'amecha b'acharit hayamim: 'I have come to make you understand what is to happen to your people in the latter days.' B'acharit hayamim (H319 + H3117, in the latter/last days): the prophetic eschatological phrase marking the vision as concerning the end of the present age — the same phrase as Gen 49:1; Num 24:14; Isa 2:2; Mic 4:1. The vision is explicitly oriented toward eschatology, not merely near-term political history. Ki od chazot layamim: 'for the vision concerns days to come.'
10.15: uvedabro immi divarim elleh natati panai artsah ve'nilmeti: 'When he spoke these words to me, I turned my face toward the ground and was speechless.' The prophetic collapse at the content of the heavenly message (not merely the appearance of the heavenly being) deepens the encounter: the revelation itself, not just the revealer, overwhelms. Nilmeti (H481, became silent/speechless) — the speechlessness of profound awe and inadequacy before divine disclosure.
10.16: venogea kamareh benei adam al-sefatai eftach peh va'adabera: 'One having the appearance of the sons of man touched my lips, and I opened my mouth and spoke.' The touch on the lips echoes Isaiah's commission (Isa 6:7 — the coal touched to Isaiah's lips), enabling prophetic speech from one who had been silenced. The restoration of prophetic voice through divine touch is the pattern: God prepares the instrument of his word by touching the very organ of speech.
10.17: veich yuchal eved Adonai zeh ledabber im-Adonai zeh: 'How can the servant of my Lord speak with this lord of mine?' The double Adonai/Adon creates a reverential rhetorical question: the servant-status of Daniel before the heavenly messenger makes speech feel impossible. The phrase eved Adonai (servant of the Lord) is also the Isaianic designation for the suffering Servant — Daniel's self-identification places him within that tradition of covenant mediation.
10.18: vayosef vayigga bi kemar'eh ha'adam vayechazqeni: 'And again one having the appearance of a man touched me and strengthened me.' The second touch — now explicitly strengthening (chazaq, H2388) rather than silencing — restores the capacity to receive and transmit the revelation. Chazaq (be strong, strengthen) will recur in 10:19 as the thematic summary of what the heavenly visitation accomplishes for the prophet: Al-tira, shalom lach, chazaq vehitchazaq (Do not fear, peace to you, be strong and strong).
10.19: al-tira ish chamudot shalom lach chazaq vehitchazaq: 'Do not be afraid, O man greatly beloved. Peace to you; be strong and be strong.' The triple commissioning formula: no fear, peace, and double chazaq. The Hiphil chazaq + Hitpael hitchazaq creates an active and reflexive pairing: both made strong (by God) and actively strengthen yourself. Shalom lach (peace to you) is the greeting of completion and wholeness — the same shalom that marks covenant restoration. Gabriel's parting gift to the prophet is not information but the disposition needed to bear it.
10.20: ata ashuv lehilachem im-sar Paras veani yotze vehinei sar Yavan ba: 'I will return to fight against the prince of Persia; and when I go out, behold, the prince of Greece will come.' The cosmic geography of heavenly-earthly correspondence: sar Paras (prince of Persia) is replaced by sar Yavan (prince of Greece/Javan) — the Macedonian empire's rise is already visible in heaven before it appears on earth. The angelic battles are the heavenly dimension of the terrestrial empires — Persia falls because its heavenly prince is defeated; Greece rises because its prince overcomes.
10.21: efes ki agid lach et-harashum bichtav emet ve'ein echad mitchazaq immi al-eleh ki im-Michael sarcem: 'But I will tell you what is inscribed in the book of truth: there is none who contends by my side against these except Michael, your prince.' Ketav emet (book/writing of truth): a heavenly register of predetermined events — the eschatological script that Gabriel reads from. Michael sarcem (Michael your prince) — Israel has its own heavenly guardian, the chief angel Michael (12:1), who alone among the heavenly princes stands with Gabriel against the opposing national angels. Israel's protection is personal and angelic.

11.1: vaani bishnat achat leDaryavesh haMadai amodeti lemachaziq ulema'oz lo: 'And in the first year of Darius the Mede, I stood up to confirm and strengthen him.' The angelic speaker continues — this verse is the end of Gabriel's explanation, linking the heavenly guardian's role to the transition from Babylonian to Median-Persian rule. The heavenly figure's reinforcing activity occurs at the empire's transition, supporting the cosmic order behind the earthly political change.
11.2: ve'attah emet agid lach hinei od shloshah melachim omdim leParas: 'And now I will tell you the truth: Behold, three more kings shall arise in Persia.' The predictive schema is historically specific: the three kings after Cyrus are Cambyses, Pseudo-Smerdis/Bardiya, and Darius I (or Cambyses, Darius I, Xerxes depending on reckoning). The fourth who is richer than all — Xerxes I (486-465 BCE) — led the massive invasion of Greece (480 BCE). Yachaziq hakol et-malkhut Yavan (stirring up all against the kingdom of Greece): Xerxes' Persian Wars are the historical turning point that made Greece the successor power.
11.3: veamad melek gibor umashel mimshel rab ve'asah kirtsono: 'And a mighty king shall arise who shall rule with great dominion and do as he wills.' Gibor (H1368, mighty one/warrior): the term applied to Alexander the Great — the single most decisive military figure of the ancient world, who conquered the Persian empire in under a decade (336-323 BCE). Ve'asah kirtsono (did according to his will) — the language of absolute uncontested sovereignty used later for Antiochus IV (v.36) and the eschatological king. Alexander as the type of the self-willed conqueror is established here.
11.4: uveoamdo tishshaver malkhuto vetechelets le'arba ruchot hashamayim: 'And as soon as he has arisen, his kingdom shall be broken and divided toward the four winds of heaven.' The sudden death of Alexander (323 BCE) at age 32 and the immediate fragmentation of his empire into four kingdoms (the Diadochi — Ptolemy in Egypt, Seleucus in Syria/East, Cassander in Macedonia, Lysimachus in Thrace) is one of the most precisely fulfillable prophecies in the OT. Lo le'acharito velo kemoshlo asher mashal: not to his descendants, nor with authority like his — Alexander left no effective successor.
11.5: veyechezaq melek haNegev: 'The king of the south shall be strong.' Melek haNegev (king of the south): Ptolemy I Soter of Egypt (305-283 BCE). Melek haTzafon (king of the north): Seleucus I Nicator (305-281 BCE) who took control of the largest Diadochi fragment. The two competing dynasties — Ptolemies vs. Seleucids — will dominate the next hundred years of political history narrated in vv. 5-35, with the land of Israel caught between them as the geographic and strategic corridor.
11.6: uveketz shanim yitchaberu: 'After some years they shall make an alliance.' The diplomatic marriage between the two dynasties: Berenice daughter of Ptolemy II married Antiochus II Theos (the Seleucid king, vv. 6-7) ca. 252 BCE. Lo tachaziq koach hazroa (she shall not retain the strength of her arm): Berenice and her father and child were all murdered when Antiochus II restored his first wife Laodice (246 BCE). Political marriage as a covenant-mechanism is exposed here as fragile — human alliances cannot sustain themselves without deeper covenantal foundations.
11.7: veamad minetzur shorsheha al-kano: 'One from the branch of her roots shall arise in his place.' Ptolemy III Euergetes, Berenice's brother, succeeded his father (Ptolemy II) and invaded the Seleucid kingdom in revenge, penetrating as far as Seleucia-on-the-Tigris (the Third Syrian War, 246-241 BCE). The image of the branch/shoot from the royal root (netzur) echoes the Isaianic netzur imagery of the Davidic shoot (Isa 11:1 — a shoot from the stump of Jesse) — though here applied to a foreign king, not messianically.
11.8: vegam eloheihem im nasichehem im-keli chemdatam kesef vezahav yishbeh Mitsrayim: 'He shall also carry off to Egypt their gods with their metal images and their precious vessels of silver and gold.' Ptolemy III is said to have brought back to Egypt many cult statues previously taken to Babylon — the return of divine images was a powerful political-theological statement in the ancient Near East (cf. Cyrus returning the temple vessels to Jerusalem, Ezra 1:7-11). The gods themselves are carried as spoil, demonstrating the political victors' control over divine protection.
11.9: uva bemamlekhet melek haNegev veshuv el-admato: 'Then the latter shall come into the realm of the king of the south but shall return to his own land.' The mutual incursions between Ptolemaic and Seleucid kingdoms — each advancing, then retreating — mark the ongoing warfare. The cyclical nature of these conflicts, each leaving the fundamental power balance unchanged, reflects the vanity of military conquest apart from the divine decree that will eventually settle all earthly kingdoms.
11.10: uvanav yitgaru veasafu hamon chayalot rabbot: 'His sons shall wage war and assemble a multitude of great forces.' Antiochus III the Great (Seleucus III's successor) resumed the Syrian Wars, advancing through Palestine in the Fourth Syrian War (219-217 BCE) until defeated by Ptolemy IV at Raphia (217 BCE). Ve'avar veshataf ve'ashuv (he shall pass through, be swept away, and return) — the military flood-metaphor for advancing armies is the Isaianic image of the Assyrian invasion (Isa 8:7-8 — overflows and passes on).
11.11: veyitmarer melek haNegev: 'The king of the south shall be enraged.' Ptolemy IV's response to Antiochus III's advance — mobilizing a large force and winning at Raphia (217 BCE). Despite the large numbers mobilized, the victory did not consolidate Egyptian strength. Ki ya'amod hamon gadol: the great multitude is given into the hand of the victor, but the victory is incomplete — Ptolemy IV failed to capitalize strategically.
11.12: venasei hehamon yarumam vilvavo: 'When he has taken away the multitude, his heart shall be exalted.' Pride following military victory — the consistent biblical dynamic where success leads to hubris (cf. Deut 8:14; 2 Chr 26:16 on Uzziah). Ptolemy IV returned to Egypt after Raphia without pressing his advantage, and his reign declined. Vehippil rebebot velo ya'oz: he shall strike down tens of thousands yet shall not prevail — the decisive victory that fails to consolidate is a recurring historical pattern.
11.13: veshav melek haTzafon vehe'emid hamon rab: 'For the king of the north shall again raise a great multitude.' Antiochus III regrouped and, after Ptolemy IV's death (204 BCE) and the accession of the child Ptolemy V, renewed his campaign — the Fifth Syrian War (202-195 BCE), capturing Palestine. Uveketz ha'ittim shanim: after the appointed time — et (H6256) as the appointed/appropriate time, the same term used for YHWH's kairos moments throughout the prophets. History moves on YHWH's timeline, not human ambition.
11.14: uveat hahi rabbim yaqumu al-melek haNegev uvnei paritsei amcha yinasu lehakim chazun venichshalu: 'In those times many shall rise against the king of the south, and the violent ones among your own people shall lift themselves up in order to fulfill the vision, but they shall fail.' Bnei paritzei amcha (the violent ones of your people): Jewish pro-Seleucid factions who collaborated with Antiochus III, believing his conquest would fulfill the restoration prophecies. Their failure — venichshalu (they stumbled/fell) — illustrates the danger of presuming to force prophetic fulfillment through political-military action.
11.15: uveshavat ir hamivtzarot velo yaamdur zeroa haNegev: 'The king of the north shall come and cast up siege works and take a well-fortified city.' Sidon fell to Antiochus III in 198 BCE; the Egyptian general Scopas was defeated at Panion and besieged at Sidon. Zeroa (H2220, arm/strength): the military arm metaphor — Egypt's arm proved insufficient against the Seleucid advance. The arm/strength of earthly powers is consistently shown as inadequate in Daniel's vision.
11.16: veyaas haba elav kirtsono ve'ein oamed lefanav: 'He who comes against him shall do as he wills, and none shall stand before him.' Antiochus III's unchecked advance through Palestine after 198 BCE. Eretz haTzvi (the beautiful/glorious land): the standard prophetic designation for the land of Israel (Ezek 20:6, 15; Jer 3:19; Dan 8:9; 11:41) — the land that becomes the arena of competing imperial ambitions throughout vv. 5-35.
11.17: veyasem panav lavo bteqeph kol malkhuto: 'He shall set his face to come with the strength of his whole kingdom.' Antiochus III's diplomatic strategy — marrying his daughter Cleopatra I to the young Ptolemy V (193 BCE), hoping she would serve his interests in Egypt. Uvat hanashim yitennah lo lehashchitah: he shall give him a daughter of women to destroy him, but it shall not stand. Cleopatra I proved loyal to Egypt, not her father. The marriage-alliance strategy — using a daughter as a political weapon — illustrates the instrumentalization of persons in power politics, which Daniel's vision exposes as ultimately futile.
11.18: veyasev panav le'iyim ulakad rabbim: 'He shall turn his face to the coastlands and shall capture many of them.' Antiochus III's western campaigns (196-191 BCE) into Asia Minor and the Aegean islands, bringing him into conflict with Rome. Veheshiv lo cherpo (a commander shall put an end to his insolence): the Roman commander Lucius Cornelius Scipio Asiaticus defeated Antiochus III at Magnesia (190 BCE) — the first decisive Roman intervention in the eastern Mediterranean, signaling Rome's future imperial dominance.
11.19: veyasev panav lema'uzzei artzo venikhshal venapal velo yimmatze: 'Then he shall turn his face back toward the fortresses of his own land, but he shall stumble and fall, and shall not be found.' Antiochus III's humiliating retreat after Magnesia, his attempt to plunder a temple in Elymais to restore his depleted treasury (187 BCE), and his assassination there — the great Seleucid king ended obscurely. The language velo yimmatze (and shall not be found) suggests sudden disappearance from history's stage — a warning to all who trust in military strength.
11.20: veamad al-kanno over nogesh heder malkhut: 'In his place one shall arise who shall send an exactor of tribute through the glory of the kingdom.' Seleucus IV Philopator (187-175 BCE) who sent Heliodorus to plunder the Jerusalem temple treasury (2 Macc 3) — the historical background for the Maccabean crisis. Uvi'yamim achadim yishaber velo ve'aph beanaf velo bemilchamah: he shall be broken within a few days, neither in anger nor in war. Heliodorus poisoned Seleucus IV — an undramatic end that nevertheless transferred power to Antiochus IV.
11.21: veamad al-kanno niv'zeh velo natenu alav hod malkhut: 'In his place shall arise a contemptible person to whom royal majesty has not been given.' Niv'zeh (H959, despised/held in contempt): the same root as Isa 53:3's niv'zeh — the despised servant. Here it describes Antiochus IV Epiphanes who was not the legitimate heir (the throne belonged to Demetrius, son of Seleucus IV, held as a Roman hostage). Uvah beshilva vehecheziq malkhut bechalaqlaqot: he shall come in time of security and obtain the kingdom by flatteries — the seizure of power through intrigue rather than legitimate succession.
11.22: uzeroa hashtetef yishtof milfanav veyishaveru: 'Armies shall be utterly swept away before him and broken, even the prince of the covenant.' Nagid berit (prince of the covenant): debated — either a Ptolemaic official or the high priest Onias III, who was removed from office by Antiochus IV's intrigues (2 Macc 4:7-10). The desacralization of the high priesthood — nagid berit (covenant-prince) eliminated — marks the beginning of Antiochus's assault on the covenantal order.
11.23: ume'hitchabro elav ya'aseh mirmah: 'After an alliance is made with him he shall act deceitfully.' Mirmah (H4820, deceit/treachery): Antiochus IV's characteristic political mode. The Ptolemaic alliance he entered only to betray marks the corruption of covenant-making — the very instrument of international relations (treaties) is weaponized through deception.
11.24: beshilvah uvimshmane medinah yavo: 'In a time of security he shall come into the richest parts of the province.' Antiochus IV's innovative strategy of winning loyalty through lavish distribution of plunder — unlike his predecessors he gave away what he had seized, buying support. Veyachashor machashavot al-mivtzarim ve'ad et: he shall devise plans against strongholds, but only for a time. The et (appointed time) limit frames all his activities within divine permission — he acts within time granted, not unlimited.
11.25: veyaer kocho ulvavo al-melek haNegev bechayil gadol: 'He shall stir up his power and his heart against the king of the south with a great army.' Antiochus IV's first Egyptian campaign (170-169 BCE). Velo yaamood ki yachashvu alav machashavot: the Egyptian king shall not stand because plots shall be devised against him. The political betrayal by Egypt's own counselors mirrors the intrigue by which Antiochus himself seized power — the corruption spreads through all parties involved.
11.26: ve'ochelei patbago yishberuhu: 'The ones who eat his food shall break him.' The betrayal by court insiders — those who benefit from the king's table become the instruments of his downfall. The image of table-fellowship as the site of betrayal echoes the psalmist's lament (Ps 41:9 — the one who ate my bread lifted his heel against me) and anticipates the Judas-betrayal pattern. Inner circles are as dangerous as external enemies.
11.27: ushneihem hamelachim libbam lemira veal shulchan echad kazav yadaberu: 'And as for the two kings, their hearts shall be bent on doing evil. They shall speak lies at the same table.' The diplomatic fiction of negotiation as cover for mutual deception — both Antiochus IV and Ptolemy VI meet publicly as allies while planning against each other. Velo titzlach ki od qetz lamoedh: but it shall not succeed, for the end is yet to be at the time appointed. The divine moedh (appointed time) overrides all human plotting.
11.28: veyashov artzo birechush gadol ulvavo al-berit qodesh: 'And he shall return to his land with great wealth, but his heart shall be set against the holy covenant.' Antiochus IV's return from Egypt (169 BCE) with enormous plunder — including the pillaging of the Jerusalem temple (1 Macc 1:20-28; 2 Macc 5:15-21). Berit qodesh (holy covenant): the first explicit mention of the covenant as the target of Antiochus's hostility — not merely geopolitical rivalry but a theological-covenantal assault. The enemy of the people of God is ultimately the enemy of their covenant.
11.29: lamoedh yashuv uvah banegev velo tihyeh kharishon vekha'acharon: 'At the time appointed he shall return and come into the south, but it shall not be this time as it was before.' The second Egyptian campaign (168 BCE) was less successful — Antiochus IV was met at Eleusis by the Roman legate Gaius Popilius Laenas, who drew a circle in the sand around Antiochus and demanded he decide on peace before stepping out of the circle. Forced to withdraw, his frustration was redirected against Jerusalem.
11.30: uva bo tziyim Kittim venikh'ah veshav veza'am al-berit qodesh: 'For ships of Kittim shall come against him, and he shall be afraid and withdraw, and shall turn back and be enraged and take action against the holy covenant.' Kittim (H3794): originally Cypress, later used for western maritime powers — here Rome. The Roman humiliation at Eleusis is the turning point: unable to vent his fury on Rome, Antiochus redirected against the covenant people. Veshav veham al-ozvei berit qodesh: he shall pay attention to those who forsake the holy covenant — the Hellenizing Jewish collaborators who aided his assault on Judaism.
11.31: uzeroa mimenu yaamodu vechill'lu et-haMiqdash haMa'oz veheisiru hatamid venatenu hashiquts meshomem: 'Forces from him shall appear and profane the temple and fortress, and shall take away the regular burnt offering and set up the abomination that makes desolate.' Hasorim hatamid (removing the regular/continual offering): Antiochus IV abolished the daily tamid sacrifice in Kislev 167 BCE (1 Macc 1:39, 45). Shiquts meshomem (abomination of desolation): the altar of Zeus Olympios erected on the altar of burnt offering (1 Macc 1:54; 2 Macc 6:2) — Jesus identifies this as the signal for the eschatological tribulation (Mark 13:14).
11.32: umarishiei berit yachanif bechalaqlaqot veyodei Elohav yacharziqu veasu: 'He shall seduce with flattery those who violate the covenant, but the people who know their God shall stand firm and take action.' Yodei Eloheihem (those who know their God): yada (H3045, know) as covenantal intimacy and experiential relationship — not merely intellectual knowledge but the deep personal knowledge of a people in covenant. Yacharziqu veasu: they shall stand firm and act — the combination of interior resolve and exterior witness marks the Maccabean faithful as the paradigm for covenant loyalty under pressure.
11.33: umaskilei am yavinu larabim venikhshalu beherev uvelehavah bishvi uvevizah yamim: 'And the wise among the people shall make many understand, though for some days they shall stumble by sword and flame, by captivity and plunder.' Maskilei am (those who make wise among the people): maskil from sakal (H7919, be prudent/give insight) — the teacher-sages who help the community understand what is happening in light of covenant history. Their ministry costs them severely — sword, flame, captivity, plunder. The teacher of truth in a hostile empire bears the cost of fidelity.
11.34: ubehibashalam yee'zeru ezra me'at venitlavu aleihem rabbim bachalaqlaqot: 'When they stumble, they shall receive a little help. And many shall join themselves to them with flattery.' Ezra me'at (a little help): the Maccabean revolt (begun 167 BCE) is described with striking understatement — not the glorious deliverance but only modest assistance, with opportunists joining insincerely. The phrase bachalaqlaqot (with flatteries) — the same root used for Antiochus's intrigue (v.21) now characterizing those who attach to the faithful cause for self-protection.
11.35: umin hamaskilim yikashlu letzaref bahem ulvarer ullilaben ad-et qetz: 'And some of the wise shall stumble, so that they may be refined, purified, and made white, until the time of the end, for it is yet for the time appointed.' The suffering of the righteous teachers is explicitly purposive: letzaref (to refine like metal), levarer (to purify), lillaben (to make white). Three metallurgical-laundering metaphors for the purification that persecution produces (cf. Mal 3:3; 1 Pet 1:7). Ki od lamo'ed: the suffering is bounded — the appointed time limits all persecution.
11.36: veyitromem veyitgadal al-kol-El veal El Elim yedabber niflaot: 'And the king shall do as he wills. He shall exalt himself and magnify himself above every god, and shall speak astonishing things against the God of gods.' Yitromem veyitgadal: both Hitpael (reflexive) verbs — self-exaltation doubled. Niflaot (H6381, wonders/monstrous things/things beyond capacity): the claim to divine status and power beyond what any human can legitimately claim. This verse transitions from the historical Antiochus IV to what many interpreters read as a typological figure — the eschatological self-divinizing power (cf. 2 Thess 2:3-4; Rev 13:5-6) who recapitulates Antiochus's pattern at history's end.
11.37: veal Elohei avotav lo yavin veal chemdath nashim: 'He shall pay no attention to the gods of his fathers, or to the one beloved by women.' Elohei avotav (gods of his fathers): abandoning ancestral religion — Antiochus IV adopted the cult of Zeus Olympios and demanded divine worship for himself. Chemdath nashim (desire/beloved of women): the most natural reading is Adonis/Tammuz, the dying-and-rising deity widely mourned by women (cf. Ezek 8:14 — women weeping for Tammuz). Antiochus's religious innovations eliminated even this popular cult. The eschatological king abandons all prior religious loyalties in pursuit of absolute self-divinization.
11.38: veal-Elohei ma'uzim al kanno yechabbed: 'He shall honor the god of fortresses instead of these.' Elohei ma'uzim (god of fortresses/strongholds): the deity of military power — possibly Mars/Ares or Jupiter Capitolinus, or simply the raw power of military force. The eschatological king worships raw power — not as means to an end but as ultimate value. This is the logic of empire stripped of its religious veneer: the only god that matters is the god who gives military victory.
11.39: ve'asah lemivatsei ma'uzim im-elohah nechar: 'He shall deal with the strongest fortresses with the help of a foreign god.' The combination of military strategy and religious innovation — using new religious incentives (yarbeh kavod, increase honor) to motivate soldiers. Vehimshilan barabim vechamat adamah yechalek bishcar: dividing the land as a bribe. The monetization of loyalty through territorial grants marks the transactional politics of totalitarian power.
11.40: ubeet qetz yitnagach imo melek haNegev: 'At the time of the end, the king of the south shall attack him.' The et qetz (time of the end) marks the transition to eschatological horizon — whether continuing the Antiochus IV narrative or moving to a future figure who recapitulates his pattern. The military flood-metaphor resumes: yishtatef ba'aratzot (overflow through many countries) — the Assyrian/Babylonian flood-image (Isa 8:7-8; Dan 9:26) applied to the final military campaign.
11.41: uvoa b'Eretz haTzvi verobot yikashelu: 'He shall come into the beautiful land, and tens of thousands shall fall.' Eretz haTzvi (the beautiful/desirable land) recurs as the focal point — the land of covenant, which becomes the eschatological battle-ground. Ve'eleh yimatlu miyado Edom uMo'av ureishit Bnei Ammon: Edom, Moab, and the firstfruits of Ammon escape — traditional Israel-enemies who are preserved, paradoxically, when Israel suffers. The selectivity of eschatological judgment preserves historical irony.
11.42: viyishllach yado ba'aratzot uEretz Mitsrayim lo tihyeh liphletah: 'He shall stretch out his hand against the countries, and the land of Egypt shall not escape.' Shalach yad (stretch out the hand) is the Exodus-language of YHWH's powerful intervention (Exod 3:20; 7:5) — the eschatological conqueror appropriates the Exodus gesture of divine action for his own military campaign, the ultimate parody of divine power.
11.43: umashol bemiqdanot hazahav vekheseph uvechol chamudot Mitsrayim: 'He shall become ruler of the treasures of gold and of silver, and all the precious things of Egypt.' The wealth of empires — gold, silver, precious things — that Daniel's vision catalogs through eleven chapters is ultimately exposed as transient. The rich nations that trusted their treasuries are stripped. Valuvim uKushim bimitzadav: the Libyans and Cushites following in his train — the widest geographic extent of the final empire's dominion, reaching Egypt and its African dependencies.
11.44: vishmuot yebahaluhu mizrach utzafon veyatze bechemah gedolah: 'But news from the east and the north shall alarm him, and he shall go out with great fury to destroy and devote many to destruction.' The reversal: at the moment of maximum expansion, alarming news from flank regions creates the conditions for the conqueror's undoing. Lehashmid ulehachriem rabbim (to destroy and devote many to destruction): the cherem (H2763, sacred destruction/ban) language of holy war — the total-destruction devoted to the deity. The final military campaign employs the vocabulary of sacred destruction, the ultimate parody of covenant warfare.
11.45: veyita oholei apadno bein yamim lehar tzevi qodesh: 'And he shall pitch his palatial tents between the sea and the glorious holy mountain.' The eschatological conqueror establishes his camp between the Mediterranean and the Temple Mount — the geographically and theologically central location of the cosmos in biblical geography. Uvah ad-qitzo ve'ein ozer lo: 'Yet he shall come to his end, with none to help him.' The three-word oracle — qitzo ve'ein ozer — is the anti-climax of the entire chapter's imperial narrative: the self-divinizing conqueror dies without help, alone, the way every absolute power ends. YHWH's moedh (appointed time) has the last word.

6.1: ʾaḥašdarpĕnayyāʾ (Aramaic: אַחַשְׁדַּרְפְּנַיָּא): the Aramaic form of 'satraps' is a loanword from Old Persian xšaθrapāvan ('protector of the kingdom/province'). Daniel's Aramaic narrative is peppered with Persian administrative vocabulary reflecting the historical setting of the Achaemenid court. The number 120 — governing the whole realm — establishes the imperial scope before Daniel's singular excellence is highlighted.
6.2: sārakîn (סָרְכִין): the three 'presidents' or 'chief supervisors' over the 120 satraps are designated by this Aramaic term, probably from Old Persian *sāraka (overseer). The administrative hierarchy (120 satraps → 3 supervisors → 1 king) frames Daniel's appointment as first among equals, setting up the jealousy that drives the plot.
6.3: rûaḥ yattîrāʾ (רוּחַ יַתִּירָא): 'an extraordinary spirit.' Yattîr (Aramaic) means 'exceeding, surpassing, preeminent' — used throughout Daniel 7 for the terrifying fourth beast ('exceedingly terrifying'). The same superlative spirit that distinguishes Daniel is the one that distinguishes the little horn's pride: the word cuts both ways. Rûaḥ is the standard Aramaic/Hebrew term for spirit, breath, and wind.
6.4: ʿillatāʾ wĕšuḥlĕtānāʾ (עִלָּתָא וְשֻׁחְלְתָנָא): 'ground/pretext and corruption.' The princes seek ʿillat — a legal pretext, cause for complaint — against Daniel. The pairing of 'ground' and 'corruption/negligence' covers the two categories of potential accusation: official error and moral failing. None was found because Daniel was mĕhêman (מְהֵימַן, 'trustworthy/faithful') — the same root as Aramaic ʾāmēn.
6.5: dāt ʾĕlāhēh (דָּת אֱלָהֵהּ): 'the law of his God.' Dāt is a Persian loanword (Old Persian dāta = law, edict) that became standard Aramaic and Hebrew administrative vocabulary (appears in Esther extensively). The conspirators' conclusion — that Daniel's only vulnerability is his religious practice — is simultaneously a testimony to his integrity and a clue about where the narrative will resolve.
6.6: haṣṣĕlîn (חַצְּלִין): the princes 'thronged' or 'came rushing' to the king. The Aramaic verb describes tumultuous, coordinated arrival. The flattery formula 'O king, live forever' (malkāʾ lĕʿālmîn ḥĕyî) is the standard Aramaic court address, translated woodenly to preserve its formal character. The greeting's irony is that Daniel will soon demonstrate what genuine eternal life looks like under divine protection.
6.7: gubb ʾaryāwātāʾ (גֻּב אַרְיָוָתָא): 'the den of lions.' Gubb means pit or cistern — the lions' den was likely a rock-cut pit, not a cage, which explains the stone-sealed opening (v. 17) and the ability to throw people in. ʾAryāwātāʾ is the emphatic plural of Aramaic aryeh/arweh (lion), cognate with Hebrew אַרְיֵה. The thirty-day petition prohibition is designed specifically to target Daniel's three-times-daily prayer.
6.8: dāt māday ûpāras (דָּת מָדַי וּפָרַס): 'the law of the Medes and Persians.' The Achaemenid principle of irrevocable royal decrees is well-attested in classical sources (Esther 1:19; 8:8). The permanent character of the edict — dî-lāʾ lĕhašnāyāh, 'which is not to be changed' — is the dramatic fulcrum of the narrative: the king who wants to save Daniel cannot legally override his own decree.
6.9: rĕšam malkāʾ dāryāweš kĕtābāʾ wĕʾĕsārāʾ: 'King Darius signed the writing and the interdict.' The narrative compression of verse 9 — a single sentence for the king's capitulation — contrasts with the extended buildup. The document vocabulary (kĕtābāʾ, writing; ʾĕsārāʾ, interdict/prohibition) is the same Aramaic administrative language used throughout Daniel 3-6 for royal decrees.
6.10: kawnĕwātēh pĕtîḥān lēh (כַּוִּינֵהּ פְּתִיחָן לֵהּ): 'his windows were open toward Jerusalem.' The open windows signal deliberate public prayer — Daniel knows the decree and continues his practice. Kawnāh is the Aramaic form of Hebrew חַלּוֹן (window). Praying toward Jerusalem three times daily echoes Solomon's dedication prayer (1 Kgs 8:46-50) and the twice-daily tamid offering; Daniel's three-fold prayer likely adds a midday intercession (Ps 55:17).
6.11: bāʿēh wĕmitḥannan (בָּעֵא וּמִתְחַנַּן): 'praying and making supplication.' Bĕʿāʾ (Aramaic) = to seek, ask, pray — the same verb used in the edict ('whoever shall ask a petition'). Mĕtḥannan from ḥnn = to be gracious, seek grace — Daniel prays for divine favor, the same quality the Psalms petition (Ps 4:1; 6:2). His prayer is simultaneously the act of civil disobedience and an act of theological confidence.
6.12: ʾĕsārāʾ (אֱסָרָא): 'the interdict/prohibition.' The princes quote the decree back to the king precisely, using the language of legal binding. The Aramaic ʾĕsar root (to bind) is cognate with Hebrew אָסַר and appears in legal contexts throughout the OT (Num 30). The binding character of the decree — designed to trap Daniel — is itself a form of ironic reversal: the king who signed the binding law cannot bind himself to save the one he wishes to protect.
6.13: bĕnê gālûtāʾ dî yĕhûd (בְּנֵי גָלוּתָא דִי יְהוּד): 'the sons of the captivity of Judah.' The princes identify Daniel by his ethnic-exile status, not by name. This framing attempts to delegitimize his standing in the Babylonian court. The irony: Daniel's identity as a captive Jew is precisely the context in which YHWH's protection will be most publicly displayed before an international audience.
6.14: šĕqal ʿal-libbēh (שְׁקַל עַל-לִבֵּהּ): 'it weighed on his heart.' The Aramaic idiom literally means 'it was heavy upon the heart' — an expression of deep distress. The king was striving (wĕhêwāh mištaddar) to deliver Daniel until sunset: the Aramaic verb mštaddar = exerting oneself, laboring. The narrative honors the king's conscience even while showing the trap he set for himself.
6.15: dāt māday ûpāras: the repeated appeal to Median-Persian law (v. 8 and v. 15) functions as a dramatic refrain. The princes force the king's hand by reminding him that the law of Medes and Persians cannot be altered — lāʾ-tĕšannêh (it shall not be changed). The theological counterpoint: while human law is irrevocable by design, YHWH's ability to deliver is unconstrained by any human decree.
6.16: ʾĕlāhāk dî ʾant-pālaḥ-lēh bĕdîrāʾ (אֱלָהָךְ דִּי אַנְתְּ פָּלַח-לֵהּ בְּדִירָה): 'your God whom you serve continually.' Pālaḥ (Aramaic) is the standard term for cultic service/worship in Daniel 3:12, 14, 17-18 and 6:16, 20 — corresponding to Hebrew עָבַד but with specifically cultic connotations. The king's words function as an involuntary confession of faith before Daniel's deliverance.
6.17: ḥatam bĕʿizzĕqattēh wĕbĕʿizzĕqat rabrĕbānôhî: 'sealed with his own signet and with the signet of his lords.' The double sealing — royal and nobles — prevents any intervention and ensures legal integrity. The sealed-stone motif parallels Christ's sealed tomb (Matt 27:66) and is reversed in the same way: divine power opens what human authority sealed shut.
6.18: lāʾ-haytî lēh dĕḥāwān (לָא-הַיְתִי לֵהּ דַּחֲוָן): 'no diversions were brought to him.' The Aramaic daḥăwān means entertainments, concubines, musical instruments — the standard royal comforts. The king cannot eat, cannot sleep, cannot be distracted. His vigil of distress over Daniel is the non-prayer of one who cannot yet pray but who holds out hope in darkness.
6.19: bĕnāgǎhāʾ bĕšiprāʾ (בְּנַגְהָא בִּשְׁפַרְפָּרָא): 'at dawn, at the first light.' Two Aramaic light-words stacked for emphasis: naggahāʾ (brightness, dawn) and šiprāʾ (first breaking light). The king rises 'at the dawn of dawn' — the coming of light as the time of deliverance is a pervasive biblical motif (Ps 30:5; 46:5; 90:14). The narrative positions deliverance precisely at the moment of sunrise.
6.20: ʾĕlāhāʾ ḥayyāʾ (אֱלָהָא חַיָּא): 'the living God.' This is the pagan king's spontaneous declaration — ʾĕlāhāʾ ḥayyāʾ in Aramaic, the exact equivalent of Hebrew הָאֱלֹהִים חַי.
